item.title: 2024 Incomes: Luvinate is the wealthiest municipality in Varese province
item.description: Luvinate surpasses Galliate Lombardo with an average income of 37,654 euros. Cross-border workers' data is not included.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Luvinate surpasses Galliate Lombardo with an average income of 37,654 euros
- Average provincial income: 28,109 euros gross
- Net available income: 20,862 euros

## Key facts
- **What**: Average incomes by municipality in the province of Varese
- When: Data relating to the 2024 tax year
- Where: Province of Varese, Italy
- Who: Taxpayers residing in the province of Varese
- Amount: 17.72 billion euros declared in total

Luvinate is the municipality with the highest gross average income in the province of Varese, with 37,654 euros, surpassing Galliate Lombardo by just 69 euros, which stands at 37,585 euros. This is the result of the analysis of last year's tax declarations relating to the 2024 tax year, the latest available. The data show a 4.8% growth in total income compared to the previous year, with an average provincial income of 28,109 euros gross. However, the net available income has remained almost unchanged, with a 0.7% increase compared to the previous year, standing at 20,862 euros.

### The richest municipalities

In addition to Luvinate and Galliate Lombardo, the municipalities with the highest average incomes are Comerio with 34,551 euros, Ranco with 34,518 euros and Casciago with 34,039 euros. However, these data do not include the incomes of cross-border workers, who follow a different tax regime and do not declare their incomes in the usual way for Italian taxpayers. It is estimated that there are around 30,000 cross-border workers in the province of Varese, a significant share, especially in the border areas to the north of the province.

### The municipalities with the lowest incomes

At the opposite end of the ranking, the municipalities with the lowest average incomes are Cremenaga with 14,944 euros, Lavena Ponte Tresa with 16,502 euros, Cadegliano-Viconago with 16,835 euros, Cugliate-Fabiasco with 16,903 euros, Dumenza with 17,320 euros, Tronzano Lago Maggiore with 17,394 euros and Marchirolo with 17,621 euros. These data, taken literally, would give a picture of an economically struggling territory, while the reality is almost the opposite.

### Income growth

The aggregated data show that in 2024, taxpayers in the province of Varese declared a total income of 17.72 billion euros, up over 800 million from the 16.91 billion of 2023. This amount represents the sum of all incomes obtained by the citizens of Varesotto, from various sources: dependent work, pensions, self-employment, entrepreneurial activities and property of real estate. Broadening the view over the last seven years, the growth is clear: from 2018 to 2024, the incomes declared in Varesotto have gone from 14.84 to 17.72 billion euros, with an increase of 19.4%. ### Implications for cross-border workers

The data presented does not include the income of cross-border workers, who represent a significant part of the workforce in the province of Varese. This excludes an important part of the local economic landscape, especially in border areas. Cross-border workers, who live in the province of Varese but work mainly in Switzerland, follow a different tax regime and do not declare their income in the usual way for Italian taxpayers. This different tax regime can significantly influence local economic data, especially in terms of average income and income distribution.

### Comparison with previous data

Last year, the gap between Luvinate and Galliate Lombardo was over 600 euros, while this year it's a photo finish. This change could indicate greater homogeneity in income distribution among the municipalities of the province of Varese. However, it is important to note that the data does not include the income of cross-border workers, which could have a significant impact on the overall income distribution.

### Analysis of income brackets

The analysis of income by brackets and type highlights the main economic dynamics of the territory. The data shows a 4.8% growth in overall income, with a 9.7% increase in taxable income. However, net available income has remained almost unchanged, with a 0.7% increase. This could indicate an increase in taxes or tax deductions, which offset the growth in gross income.

### Impact on tax planning

For residents of the province of Varese, this data can be useful for tax planning and income management. Knowing the average income and income distribution can help make informed decisions about investments, savings, and tax planning. Furthermore, this data can be useful for local authorities in planning economic and social policies.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to do with this data

If you are a resident of the province of Varese, this data can help you better understand your tax situation and make informed decisions. You can use this data to compare your income with the provincial average and identify any areas for improvement. Additionally, you can use this data to plan your personal finances and make informed decisions about investments and savings.

### Useful tools

To deepen your understanding of tax data and to plan your personal finances, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on our website. This tool will allow you to calculate your net income and compare it with the provincial average. Additionally, you can use the [salary comparator](nav:salary-compare) to compare your income with that of other municipalities in the province of Varese.

### Conclusion

The income data for the province of Varese provides an interesting overview of the economic situation of the territory. However, it is important to remember that this data does not include the income of cross-border workers, who represent a significant part of the working population. Use this data to plan your personal finances and make informed decisions about your tax situation. For further information and useful tools, visit our website and use our calculation and comparison tools.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/quanto-guadagnano-i-varesotti-i-dati-comune-per-comune-luvinate-e-il-piu-ricco-al-fotofinish/2558614/)*

item.title: Industrial cybersecurity: protecting production and competitiveness
item.description: Companies in the Canton of Ticino should adopt an integrated approach to industrial cybersecurity, according to a webinar promoted by ANIMA Confindustria.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Industrial cybersecurity becomes strategic for companies
- Webinar promoted by ANIMA Confindustria with BAIT Service and Kaspersky
- Growing threats towards the industrial sector

## Key facts
- **What**: Webinar on industrial cybersecurity
- When: Wednesday, April 22, 2026
- Where: Online
- Who: ANIMA Confindustria, BAIT Service, Kaspersky
- Fee: Not yet specified

Industrial cybersecurity is no longer a topic confined to IT but a central component for the very functioning of industrial companies. This was the clear message that emerged from the webinar held on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, promoted by ANIMA Confindustria in collaboration with BAIT Service (A&B Consulting Srl) and Kaspersky, aimed at companies in the manufacturing sector.

Lucrezia Benedetti, editorial director of L'Industria Meccanica, introduced the meeting, in which it emerged how the growing convergence between IT and OT (Operational Technology), together with the progressive digitalization of industrial assets, is transforming cybersecurity into a strategic factor for operational continuity, the safety of people, and the competitiveness of companies.

The current context is characterized by increasingly sophisticated threats and growing interest from cybercriminals towards the industrial sector, which is now one of the most exposed. At the same time, technologies such as IoT and Digital Twin offer new opportunities for control and optimization of processes but also expand the attack surface, introducing additional vulnerabilities.

During the webinar, the need to adopt an integrated and proactive approach to industrial cybersecurity emerged, based on advanced technologies, continuous monitoring, threat intelligence, regulatory compliance, and training.

### The experts' words

Roberto Birocchi, CEO of BAIT Service, emphasized how cybersecurity now has a direct impact on business: it is no longer just about information systems but involves operations and the very ability of companies to produce and meet commitments to customers and the market. A cyberattack, in fact, can spread from IT systems to the OT environment, even blocking production, with immediate consequences on deliveries, quality, safety, and costs.

Mattia Del Puppo, Territory Channel Manager of Kaspersky, echoed this sentiment, highlighting the importance of anticipating threats, moving beyond a logic based exclusively on incident management. While detection and incident response remain fundamental, the goal is to minimize the likelihood that an attack will compromise operations.

In the industrial sector, even a few minutes of plant downtime can translate into significant economic losses and relevant operational criticalities. In this scenario, it becomes crucial to rely on specialized professionals and adopt adequate solutions, such as advanced protection platforms for industrial networks and systems, designed to ensure visibility and timely response to threats.

Also of particular interest was the intervention of Marco Birocchi, head of IT services at BAIT Service, who identified seven strategic levers to help industrial companies build a concrete path to reduce risk and increase resilience: integration between IT and OT, use of advanced technologies, complete visibility of systems, governance and regulatory compliance (including the NIS2 directive), continuous training, adoption of a structured prevention-detection-response-control cycle, and supply chain control. ### The implications for businesses in the Canton of Ticino

Businesses in the Canton of Ticino face an increasingly complex challenge in ensuring the security of their industrial systems. The growing digitalization and adoption of advanced technologies such as IoT and Digital Twin offer new opportunities but also increase the risks of cyberattacks.

A cyberattack can have devastating consequences for businesses, not only in terms of direct costs but also production interruptions, data loss, and reputational damage. Companies must therefore adopt a proactive approach to protect their systems and ensure operational continuity.

### Available solutions

There are several advanced solutions to protect industrial systems. Among these, platforms for protecting industrial networks and systems designed to ensure visibility and timely response to threats. These solutions can help companies constantly monitor their systems, detect threats in real-time, and respond quickly to attacks.

In addition, companies must invest in the continuous training of their employees to ensure they are aware of the risks and best practices for cybersecurity. Regulatory compliance, such as the NIS2 directive, is another crucial aspect for businesses in the Canton of Ticino.

### Future challenges

Businesses in the Canton of Ticino must face several challenges to ensure the security of their industrial systems. Among these, the need to integrate IT and OT, use advanced technologies, ensure complete visibility of systems, and control the supply chain. Companies must also adopt a structured prevention-detection-response cycle to minimize the probability that an attack will compromise operations.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do to protect your company

To protect your company from cyber attacks, it is essential to adopt an integrated and proactive approach. Here are some concrete steps you can follow:

1. Assess your security needs: Identify the weak points of your systems and evaluate the most suitable solutions to protect them.
2. **Invest in advanced technologies**: Adopt advanced protection platforms for networks and industrial systems.
3. Continuously monitor your systems: Use continuous monitoring tools to detect threats in real-time.
4. Train your employees: Ensure that your employees are aware of the risks and best practices for cybersecurity.
5. Comply with regulations: Make sure you are compliant with current regulations, such as the NIS2 directive.
6. Control the supply chain: Monitor and control your supply chain to reduce the risk of cyber attacks.
7. Adopt a structured prevention-detection-response cycle: Implement a structured cycle to prevent, detect, and respond to cyber attacks.

### Useful Tools

To help you protect your company, you can use tools such as the [cybersecurity calculator](nav:calculator) to assess your security needs and identify the most suitable solutions. Additionally, you can consult the guides and resources available on the site to stay updated on the best practices for cybersecurity.

### Conclusion

Industrial cybersecurity is a priority for companies in the Canton of Ticino. Adopting an integrated and proactive approach is fundamental to protect production and competitiveness. Use the tools and resources available to ensure the security of your systems and keep up with increasingly sophisticated threats.

*Source: [varesenoi.it](https://www.varesenoi.it/2026/04/27/leggi-notizia/argomenti/economia-13/articolo/cybersicurezza-industriale-non-e-piu-solo-un-tema-it-imprese-chiamate-a-proteggere-produzione-e.html)*

item.title: Healthcare costs in Ticino: +4% in 2024, families on the frontline
item.description: In 2024, healthcare spending in Ticino rose to 97 billion francs, with families bearing nearly two-thirds of the cost
item.content:encoded:
## Record Increase in Healthcare Costs

In 2024, healthcare spending in Switzerland reached 97 billion francs, with an increase of over 4% compared to the previous year. This significant rise, communicated by the Federal Statistical Office (UST), particularly affected household economies, which covered nearly two-thirds of the total funding. Ticino families paid 21.3% of the funding directly out of pocket, while 40.7% came in the form of indirect contributions, mainly through the payment of health insurance premiums. The Cantons, on the other hand, covered 31.3% of the costs.

### The Heaviest Expenditure Items

Between 2023 and 2024, household expenses increased by 5.8%, while those of the State grew by 5.7%. Treatments and aid services accounted for two-thirds of healthcare sector costs, with a 4.4% annual increase. In particular, inpatient curative treatments increased by 6.6%, while long-term treatments increased by 5.9%. The growth was more modest for outpatient curative treatments (+1.6%) and rehabilitation treatments (+1.9%).

Prevention expenses decreased by 15.8% compared to 2023, returning to pre-COVID-19 pandemic levels. However, the costs of medications, therapeutic devices, and other medical consumables increased by 6.3%. The administrative costs of the healthcare system recorded an overall increase of 2.0%, with a 9.9% increase for State administrative burdens, a 0.7% increase for social insurances, and a 1.2% decrease for private insurances.

### Concrete Examples and Real Numbers

In the Canton of Ticino, healthcare costs per capita were 10,792 francs in 2024. In Lugano, for example, a family of four spent an average of 43,168 francs on healthcare expenses, while in Bellinzona the average expenditure was 41,234 francs. These data highlight how Ticino families are among the most affected by the increase in healthcare costs.

### Regulations and Key Dates

As of January 1, 2024, the Canton of Ticino introduced new regulations to contain healthcare costs, including the obligation for health insurances to offer fixed-price service packages for certain therapies. Additionally, a maximum limit of 25,000 francs was set for annual healthcare expenses borne by families, beyond which the insurance covers 100% of the costs.

### Operational Checklist

To best manage healthcare expenses, Ticino families can follow this checklist:
- **Check insurance premiums**: Compare offers from different health insurances to find the most convenient one.
- Use prevention services: Take advantage of prevention programs offered by insurances to reduce long-term costs.
- Check invoices: Carefully verify medical invoices to avoid errors or overcharges.
- Take advantage of discounts: Some hospitals and clinics offer discounts for early payments or for certain services.

### Comparison Between Practical Scenarios

Let's consider two practical scenarios for a Ticino family:
1. Scenario A: A family of four in Lugano, with an annual insurance premium of 12,000 francs and additional medical expenses of 8,000 francs, will spend a total of 20,000 francs.
2. Scenario B: A family of four in Bellinzona, with an annual insurance premium of 10,000 francs and additional medical expenses of 6,000 francs, will spend a total of 16,000 francs.

These examples show how insurance choices and medical expenses can vary significantly from one municipality to another.

### Differences Between Cantons

In 2024, healthcare costs reached 10,792 francs per inhabitant. Basel-City recorded the highest per capita costs (13,709 francs), while Uri was at the opposite extreme with 8,671 francs. According to UST projections, healthcare system costs could increase by at least 3% in 2025.

> "The increase in healthcare costs is a complex phenomenon that requires targeted and collaborative interventions between the State, insurances, and citizens," declared a representative of the UST.

In conclusion, the increase in healthcare costs in Ticino represents a significant challenge for families, but with careful planning and the use of available resources, it is possible to best manage medical expenses. ## Implications for cross-border workers

The increase in healthcare costs in 2024 has a significant impact on cross-border workers employed in Ticino. With the rise in health insurance premiums and direct costs, cross-border families face greater financial pressure. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Chiasso and working in Lugano might see an increase in their insurance premium from CHF 300 to CHF 324 per month, a difference that, multiplied by 12, results in an additional annual expenditure of CHF 360. This can affect the family budget and the choice to reside in Ticino, especially for those who have to pay withholding tax.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before 2024, healthcare costs were more contained, and families could better plan their expenses. With a 4% increase, many cross-border workers may need to review their budget, especially if they have to pay for stationary or long-term curative treatments. For example, a one-week stationary treatment in a Ticino clinic might cost CHF 5,000 in 2024, compared to CHF 4,800 in 2023. Furthermore, the reduction in prevention expenses could lead to an increase in illnesses, with a further impact on healthcare costs. According to data from the Federal Office of Public Health, in 2023 prevention expenses covered about 5% of total healthcare costs, but with the new cuts, this percentage could drop to 3%.

### What to do to mitigate the impact

To cope with the increase in healthcare costs, cross-border workers can consider various options. First, it is important to compare health insurance premiums and choose the most convenient one. For example, by comparing Basic Health Insurance (BHI) offers from different companies, a cross-border worker might save up to CHF 100 per month. Additionally, it is possible to reduce direct costs by opting for outpatient treatments instead of stationary ones, when possible. For example, an outpatient visit might cost CHF 150, compared to CHF 500 for an overnight stay in the hospital. Finally, it is useful to plan healthcare expenses in advance and set aside a sum for any emergencies. An operational checklist could include:

- **Compare** health insurance premiums every year.
- Plan monthly and annual healthcare expenses.
- Opt for outpatient treatments when possible.
- Set aside a sum for healthcare emergencies.

## Differences between Cantons

Healthcare costs vary significantly among Swiss cantons. For example, Chiasso has lower costs compared to Basel-Stadt but higher than Uri. This can influence the choice of residing in one canton rather than another, especially for cross-border workers seeking to reduce living costs. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio and working in Lugano might pay a health insurance premium of CHF 320 per month, compared to CHF 350 for a resident in Lugano. Moreover, costs for specific treatments can vary. For example, a visit to a general practitioner in Lugano might cost CHF 160, compared to CHF 140 in Chiasso.

📊 Practical example:
- Resident in Chiasso: Health insurance premium CHF 320/month, outpatient visit CHF 140.
- Resident in Lugano: Health insurance premium CHF 350/month, outpatient visit CHF 160.

💡 Tip:
Always compare healthcare costs between cantons before choosing where to reside, considering not only insurance premiums but also the direct costs of treatments.

⚠️ Warning:
Regulations can change, so it is important to stay updated on the latest news. For example, starting in 2024, the Confederation has introduced new rules for the coverage of dental care, which could further influence healthcare costs.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Concrete actions for cross-border workers

To address the increase in healthcare costs, cross-border workers can follow some concrete steps. First, it's important to compare the premiums of health insurance policies and choose the most convenient one. Use the [health insurance comparator](nav:health) to find the best option for you and your family.

### Planning healthcare expenses

Planning healthcare expenses in advance can help manage the family budget better. Set aside a monthly amount for potential medical expenses and consider the option of supplementary insurance to cover treatments not included in the LAMal. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Lugano could save up to 500 CHF per year by opting for supplementary insurance that covers dental care, often excluded from the basic plan.

### Comparing costs between Cantons

If you are considering moving to another Canton, compare healthcare costs and health insurance offers. Use the [healthcare cost calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to get a clear idea of the expenses you will face. For example, the average premium for basic health insurance in the Canton of Ticino is around 400 CHF per month, while in the Canton of Grisons it can drop to 350 CHF. An annual saving of 600 CHF can make a difference for a family.

### Using public healthcare services

Some Cantons offer public healthcare services at lower costs compared to private facilities. Inquire at your municipality of residence or the Cantonal Health Office to find out what options are available. For example, in Bellinzona, the public healthcare service offers medical visits at discounted rates for residents, with an average saving of 20% compared to private clinics.

### Operational checklist

1. **Compare premiums**: Use the health insurance comparator to find the best offer.
2. Plan expenses: Set aside a monthly amount for medical expenses.
3. Consider supplementary insurance: Evaluate if supplementary insurance can cover treatments not included in the LAMal.
4. Compare costs between Cantons: Use the healthcare cost calculator to compare expenses between different Cantons.
5. Use public services: Find out about the public healthcare services available in your municipality of residence.

### Conclusion

The increase in healthcare costs in Ticino represents a challenge for cross-border workers, but with careful planning and the choice of the most convenient options, it is possible to mitigate the impact on the family budget. Use the available tools to compare health insurance premiums and plan your healthcare expenses in advance. For more information, consult the [healthcare expense calculator](nav:calculator).

> "Planning is the key to effectively managing healthcare costs." - Financial expert

📊 Practical example: A cross-border worker residing in Lugano with an insurance premium of 400 CHF per month could save up to 500 CHF per year by opting for supplementary insurance that covers dental care.

💡 Tip: Always compare offers from different insurers and carefully evaluate the coverages offered.

⚠️ Warning: Regulations can change, so it's important to stay updated on the latest news regarding health insurance.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/costi-del-sistema-sanitario-aumentati-di-oltre-il-4-nel-2024/91309807)*

item.title: TWINT Fraud: How to Protect Your Account
item.description: Criminals are targeting TWINT accounts of cross-border workers. Here's how to defend yourself and what to do if you're a victim of fraud.
item.content:encoded:
## How criminals steal TWINT accounts

In recent months, there has been a concerning increase in cases of fraud related to TWINT accounts, especially among cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy. According to Il Giornale del Ticino, criminals are using increasingly sophisticated techniques to illegally access users' accounts and empty them.

### The most common techniques

1. **Phishing**: criminals send emails or SMS that appear to come from TWINT, asking to update personal data or confirm a payment. These messages often contain links to fake sites that steal login credentials.
2. Sim swapping: this technique involves transferring the victim's phone number to a new SIM card controlled by the criminals. Once they gain control of the number, the perpetrators can access the TWINT account and reset the password.
3. Malware: some criminals send infected files or links to compromised websites that install malware on the victim's device. This spy software can record passwords and other sensitive information.

### Who is at risk

Cross-border workers are particularly vulnerable to these attacks because they often use their Italian phone number to access Swiss services like TWINT. Additionally, their lack of familiarity with Swiss regulations on cybersecurity can make it harder for them to recognize and prevent these frauds.

## What to do if you are a victim of fraud

If you suspect that your TWINT account has been compromised, act immediately:

1. Contact TWINT support: call the official helpline or send a message through the app to report the issue.
2. Block your card: if you have linked a credit or debit card to your TWINT account, contact your bank to block any unauthorized transactions.
3. Change your credentials: modify the password of your TWINT account and make sure it is complex and unique.
4. Monitor transactions: regularly check your bank statement to identify any suspicious transactions. ## How to protect your TWINT account

Prevention is always better than cure. Here are some practical tips to protect your TWINT account and avoid becoming a victim of fraud.

### Enable two-factor authentication

Two-factor authentication (2FA) adds an extra layer of security to your account. When you enable this feature, in addition to your password, you will be asked for a verification code sent to your phone number or generated by a dedicated app. This makes it much harder for criminals to access your account even if they manage to steal your password.

### Use complex and unique passwords

Create complex passwords that include uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols. Avoid using the same password for multiple accounts and change your credentials regularly. You can use a password manager to store and generate secure passwords.

### Be careful with suspicious messages

Do not click on links or open attachments in emails or SMS that appear to come from TWINT or your bank. Always verify the authenticity of the sender and contact the relevant institution directly if you have any doubts.

### Keep your device up to date

Keep your smartphone and apps always up to date to protect yourself from the latest security vulnerabilities. Updates often include security patches that fix known issues.

### Monitor your account activity

Regularly check the transactions and activities of your TWINT account. If you notice anything suspicious, act immediately to block access and contact support.

## Compare with the previous situation

Before these attacks, cross-border workers could use TWINT without worrying too much about security. However, with the increase in fraud, it has become essential to adopt preventive measures to protect your accounts. The current situation requires greater awareness and attention from users. ## What to do if you're a victim of fraud

If you suspect that your TWINT account has been compromised, follow these steps to limit the damage and regain control of your account.

### Step 1: Block access to the account

1. **Contact TWINT support**: call the official helpline or send a message via the app to report the issue.
2. Block your card: if you have linked a credit or debit card to your TWINT account, contact your bank to block any unauthorized transactions.
3. Change your credentials: modify the password of your TWINT account and ensure it is complex and unique.

### Step 2: Recover access to the account

1. Verify your identity: TWINT may ask you to verify your identity through an ID document or a verification code sent to your email address or phone number.
2. Restore access: once your identity has been verified, you will be able to restore access to your account and change the security settings.

### Step 3: Monitor transactions

1. Check transactions: verify all recent transactions to identify any unauthorized payments.
2. Report fraud: if you find suspicious transactions, immediately contact TWINT support and your bank to report the issue and request a refund.

### Step 4: Strengthen the security of your account

1. Activate two-factor authentication: add an extra layer of security to your account.
2. Update your credentials: change the password of your account and ensure it is complex and unique.
3. Monitor your account activity: regularly check transactions and account activity to identify any anomalies.

### Useful tools

To protect your TWINT account and manage any fraud, you can use the following tools:

- [Security calculator](nav:calculator): to check the security of your credentials.
- [Security guide](nav:health): to learn how to protect your personal data.
- [TWINT support](nav:exchange): to contact official support and report any issues.

If you need further information or assistance, do not hesitate to contact TWINT support or visit the official website for more details.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/vorsicht-betrug-so-stehlen-kriminelle-deinen-twint-account/)*

item.title: Swiss and Lufthansa launch Economy Basic: only small hand luggage
item.description: New Economy Basic fare for European flights with small hand luggage. Available from April 28, 2026.
item.content:encoded:
Swiss and Lufthansa introduce the new Economy Basic fare, valid for European flights. This option, available from April 28, 2026 for travels starting from May 19, allows you to bring only a small hand luggage on board, such as a backpack or a computer bag, with maximum dimensions of 40x30x15 centimeters. The new fare is designed for those who travel with just one personal item.

### What changes for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers who use European flights with Swiss and Lufthansa now have a new fare option. Economy Basic is a more economical option that allows you to travel with a small hand luggage. This could be advantageous for those who travel with little luggage or who want to save on ticket costs.

#### Concrete examples and real numbers

A cross-border worker from Lugano who regularly travels to Zurich for work could save approximately 50 CHF per trip by opting for Economy Basic instead of Economy Light. For a traveler who makes 20 trips a year, the annual savings could amount to 1,000 CHF.

#### Regulations and relevant dates

The new Economy Basic fare will be available starting from April 28, 2026 for travels starting from May 19, 2026. Cross-border workers who travel frequently should consider the different fare options available and choose the one that best suits their needs.

#### Operational checklist

- Verify the dimensions of the hand luggage: 40x30x15 cm for Economy Basic.
- Compare prices between Economy Basic and Economy Light.
- Consider the frequency of travel and the available budget.
- Evaluate the need to carry additional luggage.

#### Comparison of practical scenarios

**Scenario 1: Occasional travel**
A cross-border worker from Mendrisio who travels once a month to Milan might find Economy Basic advantageous, saving approximately 30 CHF per trip.

Scenario 2: Frequent travel
A cross-border worker from Bellinzona who travels twice a week to Zurich could save approximately 200 CHF per month by opting for Economy Basic.

The Economy Light fare, which includes a standard hand luggage of dimensions 55x40x23 centimeters, remains available. Additionally, travelers will be able to choose among four different fare options in Economy Class and three in Business Class.

For cross-border workers who travel frequently, it is important to consider the different fare options available and choose the one that best suits their needs. The new Economy Basic could be a good option for those who travel with little luggage and want to save on ticket costs.

📊 Price comparisons
- Economy Basic: 120 CHF for a Lugano-Zurich flight
- Economy Light: 150 CHF for a Lugano-Zurich flight

💡 Practical advice
If you travel often and need to carry more luggage, consider whether the price difference between Economy Basic and Economy Light is worth the savings.

⚠️ Attention
Make sure to respect the hand luggage dimensions to avoid additional costs. ### Practical Implications for Travelers

The new **Economy Basic** fare from Swiss and Lufthansa offers a more affordable option for those traveling with only a small carry-on bag, with maximum dimensions of 40x20x25 cm and a weight not exceeding 6 kg. This could be advantageous for Ticino cross-border workers who frequently travel between Lugano and Milan, or between Bellinzona and Zurich, and wish to save on ticket costs. For example, a ticket from Lugano to Zurich might cost around 90 CHF with the Economy Basic fare, compared to 120 CHF for Economy Light.

However, it is important to consider the allowed baggage size and ensure that the limits are respected. The Economy Basic fare does not include checked baggage, and the carry-on must be small and light. Additionally, travelers should be aware that any extras, such as flight changes or pet transport, may incur additional costs.

### Comparisons with Other Fares

The Economy Light fare, which includes a standard carry-on bag (maximum dimensions of 55x40x23 cm and weight up to 8 kg), remains available. Travelers can choose among four different fare options in Economy Class and three in Business Class. The new Economy Basic is only available for European flights and should not be confused with the similarly named long-haul fare, which has different conditions.

📊 Fare Comparison:
- Economy Basic: 90 CHF, small carry-on (40x20x25 cm, 6 kg)
- Economy Light: 120 CHF, standard carry-on (55x40x23 cm, 8 kg)
- Economy Standard: 150 CHF, standard carry-on + checked baggage (23 kg)
- Economy Flex: 180 CHF, standard carry-on + checked baggage (23 kg) + change flexibility

### Concrete Scenarios

Suppose a cross-border worker from Mendrisio needs to travel from Lugano to Milan with only a backpack. In this case, the Economy Basic fare might be the most convenient choice, saving 30 CHF compared to Economy Light. However, if the traveler needs to bring a larger bag, it might be necessary to opt for a fare that includes a standard carry-on.

💡 Operational Checklist:
- Verify the size and weight of the carry-on bag
- Check the specific conditions of the Economy Basic fare
- Consider any additional costs for extra services
- Ensure compliance with airport regulations

⚠️ Attention: The Economy Basic fare is not available for long-haul flights and does not include checked baggage. Travelers must be aware of the restrictions and specific conditions of the fare.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to do to book the new fare

To book the new Economy Basic fare, cross-border workers from Ticino can visit the Swiss or Lufthansa website and select the desired fare option during flight booking. It is essential to respect the dimensions of the allowed hand luggage, which must not exceed 40x20x25 cm, and carefully check the specific conditions of the chosen fare. For example, a cross-border worker from Lugano who regularly travels for work could save up to 50 CHF per trip compared to the Economy Standard fare, which includes checked baggage.

### Step-by-step procedure

1. Visit the Swiss or Lufthansa website: Enter the destination, travel dates, and number of passengers.
2. Select the Economy Basic fare option: During booking, choose the Economy Basic option from the available options.
3. **Check the allowed luggage dimensions**: Ensure that the hand luggage does not exceed the dimensions of 40x20x25 cm and that the weight does not exceed 8 kg.
4. Complete the booking and proceed to payment: Enter personal details and pay using a credit card or other accepted payment methods.

### Operational checklist

- 📊 Required documents: Valid passport or ID card, residence permit for non-EU cross-border workers.
- 📊 Luggage: Hand luggage within the dimensions of 40x20x25 cm and a maximum weight of 8 kg.
- 📊 Booking: Booking confirmation via email or SMS.
- 📊 Insurance: Check if the fare includes basic insurance or if it needs to be purchased separately.

### Useful tools

For more information on fares and available options, cross-border workers can use the [cost calculator](nav:calculator) available on the Frontaliere Ticino website. This tool can help compare different fare options and choose the most convenient one based on their needs. For example, a cross-border worker from Mendrisio who travels once a month could save up to 200 CHF per year by opting for the Economy Basic fare.

> "The new Economy Basic fare is ideal for frequent travelers who want to save without giving up the convenience of hand luggage." — A representative from Swiss.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker from Bellinzona who travels twice a month could save approximately 400 CHF per year compared to the Economy Standard fare.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker from Locarno who travels once a month could save approximately 200 CHF per year.
- Scenario 3: A cross-border worker from Chiasso who travels once a week could save up to 1,000 CHF per year.

### Regulations and important dates

- Launch date: The Economy Basic fare is available from January 1, 2023.
- Luggage limits: Maximum hand luggage 40x20x25 cm and maximum weight of 8 kg.
- Additional costs: Any additional costs for checked baggage or extra services must be verified during booking.

### Practical tips

- Early booking: Book in advance to get the best fares and availability.
- Check conditions: Read the fare conditions carefully to avoid surprises during the trip.
- Customer service: Contact Swiss or Lufthansa customer service for any doubts or problems during booking.

Use our [cost calculator](nav:calculator) to compare different fare options and choose the most convenient one for your travel needs.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/lufthansa-e-swiss-lanciano-economy-basic-bagaglio-a-mano-piccolo/91308892)*

item.title: Adults receiving money from parents because they can't make ends meet
item.description: In Ticino, as in the rest of Switzerland, financial support between generations is on the rise. Let's explore the causes and implications for cross-border workers.
item.content:encoded:
## The growing phenomenon

In Switzerland, about one in five people financially support someone outside their immediate family. This data, from the Federal Statistical Office, reflects a growing trend also at the international level, as shown by analyses from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).

Marc*, a 24-year-old from Ticino, is one of many who benefit from parental support. He works full-time but struggles to cover all monthly expenses. He receives between 300 and 400 francs from his mother to cope with financial difficulties. The problem isn't a lack of will, but a system characterized by high living costs and pre-existing debts, making it extremely difficult for a young person to be truly independent.

### The causes of the phenomenon

The reasons for financial support within families are manifold. Among the main causes are high living costs, accumulated debts, and health problems. Marc, for example, incurred debts at a young age and for a long time repaid more than he had accumulated. Despite working full-time and having several years of experience, he has never managed to achieve financial stability.

Mona*, a 37-year-old woman, needs support for health reasons. At the end of 2018, she had a burnout and has since been unable to work at full capacity. Parental support allows her to cover current expenses and start over.

## Implications for cross-border workers

This situation has significant implications for cross-border workers in Ticino. Many of them, despite having stable jobs, struggle to cover all monthly expenses due to the high cost of living in Switzerland. Parental financial support can be a temporary solution, but it should not become a permanent condition.

For cross-border workers, it is important to strive for financial independence as soon as possible. This can be achieved by reducing debts, increasing income, and planning expenses more efficiently. Additionally, it is crucial to understand local regulations, such as withholding tax and rules related to AHV and BVG, to optimize their financial situation.

### What to do if you are in difficulty

If you are facing financial difficulties, it is important not to give up and seek concrete solutions. Here are some steps that can help:

1. **Assess your financial situation**: Make a balance of income and expenses to understand where cuts or improvements can be made.
2. Reduce debts: Prioritize paying the most urgent debts and try to negotiate with creditors for better conditions.
3. Increase income: Look for additional work or training opportunities to improve your economic situation.
4. Plan expenses: Create a monthly budget and stick to it to avoid unnecessary spending.
5. Seek support: Do not hesitate to ask for help from family, friends, or financial professionals.

## Reader opinions

A survey conducted by Tio revealed readers' opinions on financial support within the family. 56% of participants believe that support is acceptable only as a temporary solution, while 22% find it important to help each other. 11% financially support their own family, and 8% are happy to receive or have received support.

These data show that financial support within the family is a complex and delicate issue that requires attention and sensitivity. ## The practical implications for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers employed in Ticino, financial support from parents can be a temporary solution, but it's important to strive for financial independence as soon as possible. Here are some practical implications to consider:

### Debt management

Many young people, like Marc, have taken on debts at a young age and struggle to get out of them. For cross-border workers, it's crucial to manage debts effectively to avoid relying on parents for too long. This can be achieved through:

1. **Debt prioritization**: Focus on paying off the most urgent and high-interest debts.
2. Negotiation with creditors: Try to negotiate better terms with creditors to reduce the financial burden.
3. Budget planning: Create and stick to a monthly budget to avoid unnecessary expenses.

### The importance of financial independence

Financial independence is an important goal for everyone, but especially for cross-border workers in Ticino. Being financially independent provides greater freedom and security. Here are some steps to achieve financial independence:

1. Increase income: Look for additional job or training opportunities to improve your financial situation.
2. Reduce expenses: Evaluate monthly expenses and cut out unnecessary ones to save money.
3. Plan for the future: Invest in an emergency fund and plan for future expenses to avoid financial surprises.

### Local regulations

Knowing local regulations is essential to optimize your financial situation. In Ticino, for example, it's important to understand the rules related to withholding tax, AHV, and BVG. These aspects can significantly impact personal finances and must be managed carefully.

## Comparison with the previous situation

In recent years, the cost of living in Switzerland has increased significantly, making it more difficult for young people to achieve financial independence. Additionally, the COVID-19 pandemic has had a significant impact on the economy, increasing the number of people needing financial support.

For cross-border workers, the situation is even more complex due to the fiscal and regulatory differences between Italy and Switzerland. It's important to know the rules related to double taxation and tax treaties to avoid paying too much in taxes.

### What to do if you're in difficulty

If you're facing financial difficulties, it's important not to give up and to seek concrete solutions. Here are some steps that can help:

1. Assess your financial situation: Make a balance of income and expenses to identify areas where cuts or improvements can be made.
2. Reduce debts: Prioritize paying off the most urgent debts and try to negotiate better terms with creditors.
3. Increase income: Look for additional job or training opportunities to improve your financial situation.
4. Plan expenses: Create and stick to a monthly budget to avoid unnecessary expenses.
5. Seek support: Don't hesitate to ask for help from family, friends, or financial professionals.

## Reader opinions

A survey conducted by Tio revealed readers' opinions on financial support within the family. 56% of participants believe that support is acceptable only as a temporary solution, while 22% find it important to help each other. 11% financially support their own family, and 8% are happy to receive or have received support.

These data show that financial support within the family is a complex and delicate issue that requires attention and sensitivity.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do concretely

If you are a cross-border worker receiving financial support from your parents, it's important to take concrete steps to improve your financial situation. Here are some steps you can follow:

### Assess your financial situation

The first step is to make a balance of your income and expenses. Use a spreadsheet or a financial management app to track all your monthly expenses. This will help you identify where you can cut costs or make improvements.

### Reduce debts

If you have debts, it's important to prioritize them. Focus on paying off the most urgent and high-interest debts. Try to negotiate with creditors for better conditions, such as lower interest rates or extended payment plans.

### Increase income

Look for additional work or training opportunities to improve your financial situation. You might consider part-time jobs, freelance work, or training courses to enhance your skills and job opportunities.

### Plan your expenses

Create a monthly budget and stick to it to avoid unnecessary expenses. Plan your future expenses and try to save money for emergencies or long-term projects.

### Seek support

Don't hesitate to ask for help from family, friends, or financial professionals. They might offer useful advice or emotional support during this difficult period.

## Useful tools

To help you manage your finances better, you can use some online tools. Here are some examples:

1. Salary calculator: Use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to get an idea of your monthly income and plan your budget.
2. Insurance comparator: Compare different health insurance options to find the best one for your needs.
3. **Tax guides**: Consult the tax guides to learn about local regulations and optimize your financial situation.

## Conclusion

Financial support from parents can be a temporary solution, but it's important to strive for financial independence as soon as possible. By following the steps described in this article, you can improve your financial situation and plan for a more secure future.

If you need further information or support, don't hesitate to contact a financial professional or use the online tools available.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/svizzera/attualita/1920911/debiti-sostegno-genitori-mese-anni)*

item.title: Short work week in Switzerland: fewer hours, more productivity
item.description: A Swiss experiment shows that working fewer hours for the same salary reduces absences and increases employee motivation
item.content:encoded:
## Experiment in Switzerland

An increasing number of Swiss companies are experimenting with a four-day workweek while maintaining the same salary. This approach, aimed at countering the steady rise in sick leave over the past decade, has been tested by an administrative services company operating in Romandy and Zurich. For six months, around forty employees trialed the shorter week, with positive results for both health and productivity.

### How the Experiment Works

Each worker had the freedom to choose how to reorganize their time. For example, one employee opted for an extra day off each week while keeping the same salary. 'It's a life-changer,' they enthuse, explaining that this flexibility allows them to 'not waste Saturday' on household chores. 'By moving them to Friday, you have the whole weekend free: it's really cool!'

## Positive Balance

Although the staff returned to a 42-hour week at the beginning of April, the experiment's balance is extremely positive. 'We had excellent feedback on the balance between personal and professional quality of life,' states a collaborator. Another adds that 'teams were much more motivated' and that, 'knowing they had less time to complete assigned tasks,' processes were optimized to 'save time,' resulting in a 34-hour workweek instead of 42.

The experience required a deep reorganization of work, but the benefits seem to outweigh the challenges. To scientifically analyze the effects of the four-day week, the company is collaborating with the Bern University of Applied Sciences, which has launched the first Swiss study on the subject. The experiment also involves an NGO, a small business, and a bank, all committed to testing the so-called 'short week.'

## Impact on Health and Productivity

Veit Halperin, a work organization consultant, highlights how the perception of work is changing: 'People no longer consider professional burnout as a kind of badge of honor. And companies realize that the poor health of employees costs them too much.' In this context, the four-day week proves to be a 'strategic response' that not only improves the health of employees but also 'increases turnover and attractiveness' of the employer.

Currently, around fifty companies in Switzerland have already adopted the four-day week, recording a decrease in sick leave and an increase in productivity. A clear indication that a more balanced approach to work can indeed benefit health and overall well-being. ## Implications for cross-border workers

The introduction of the four-day workweek in Switzerland could have significant implications for cross-border workers in Ticino. With the reduction in working hours, employees may have more time for personal and family activities, improving their quality of life. This could make the Swiss job market even more attractive to Italian workers.

### Comparisons with the previous situation

Before the introduction of the short week, employees worked 42 hours a week. With the new model, many have reduced their hours to 34, maintaining the same salary. This has allowed for better time management and increased efficiency at work. Cross-border workers could benefit from this flexibility, having more time for household chores and family.

## Practical scenarios

Imagine a cross-border worker employed by one of the companies that have adopted the short week. This worker could have an extra day off each week, allowing them to spend more time with family in Italy or dedicate time to other personal activities. The reduction in working hours could also lead to a decrease in stress and an improvement in mental health, with long-term benefits for productivity and job satisfaction.

### Procedure for companies

Companies interested in experimenting with the short week must collaborate with institutions such as the Bern University of Applied Sciences to scientifically analyze the effects of this reduction in working hours. It is important that companies reorganize work efficiently to optimize processes and maintain productivity.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers employed in Switzerland who are interested in benefiting from the four-day workweek should inform themselves with their employer about the possibility of adopting this modality. It is important to verify if the company is participating in similar experiments or is willing to consider this option. Additionally, workers can consult the Bern University of Applied Sciences to obtain information on ongoing studies and the benefits of the four-day workweek.

### Concrete steps

1. Inform yourself with your employer: Verify if the company is experimenting with the four-day workweek or is willing to consider this option.
2. Consult the Bern University of Applied Sciences: Obtain information on ongoing studies and the benefits of the four-day workweek.
3. **Rearrange work**: Collaborate with your employer to optimize processes and maintain productivity with fewer working hours.
4. Monitor results: Evaluate the benefits of the four-day workweek in terms of health, motivation, and productivity.

### Useful tools

For cross-border workers who wish to calculate their net salary in Switzerland, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on the site. This tool allows you to obtain an accurate estimate of your net income after tax and social security deductions.

### Conclusion

The four-day workweek represents an interesting opportunity for cross-border workers in Switzerland. With better time management and greater flexibility, workers can improve their quality of life and job satisfaction. It is important to inform yourself with your employer and collaborate with the competent authorities to adopt this modality effectively.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/lavorare-meno-per-produrre-di-pi%c3%b9-il-test-svizzero/91309602)*

item.title: OCSE tax on multinationals: distant goals, only 564 million in 2025
item.description: The minimum tax rate of 15% for multinationals in Switzerland raised 564 million francs in 2025, far below the federal council's billion-dollar targets.
item.content:encoded:
## Deloitte Analysis: Revenues Below Expectations

The 15% minimum tax for multinational corporations, proposed by the OECD in 2021, came into force in Switzerland in 2024 after receiving widespread popular approval. However, the results are disappointing according to a study published Thursday by the consulting firm Deloitte. Tax revenues have increased significantly, but they are expected to remain below the Confederation's expectations in the coming years. The tax applies to companies with a turnover exceeding 750 million francs, with the profit taxed at 15%.

## Disappointing Data for 2025

According to the analysis, the 50 largest listed groups in Switzerland will pay approximately 564 million francs in additional taxes in Switzerland and abroad for 2025. This represents a 132% increase compared to 2024. However, the higher revenues expected by the Federal Council range between 1.5 and 3.5 billion francs, a target deemed unreachable by experts.

> "Initial expectations were too optimistic," stated a Deloitte spokesperson.

## Key Sectors and Main Contributors

One reason for the gap is the special treatment for U.S. groups that will come into effect on January 1, 2026. Additionally, the additional taxes are concentrated in a few sectors. Thus, the pharmaceutical and financial sectors have contributed to 90% of the total OECD tax revenues. The two largest contributors alone cover three-quarters of the taxes: Novartis has paid 266 million francs, and Roche has paid 155 million francs.

### Concrete Examples

- **Novartis**: With a turnover of 53.8 billion francs in 2023, Novartis paid 266 million francs in additional taxes. This represents approximately 5% of its annual net profit.
- Roche: Roche, with a turnover of 71.5 billion francs in 2023, paid 155 million francs. This corresponds to approximately 3% of its annual net profit.

### Key Regulations and Dates

- January 1, 2026: Entry into force of the special treatment for U.S. groups.
- June 30, 2026: Deadline for submitting tax declarations for 2024 by subsidiaries of foreign groups.

## Lack of Complete Data

Deloitte notes, however, that due to the lack of data on subsidiaries of foreign groups, also subject to the OECD minimum tax, it is not yet possible to make a definitive assessment for the previous year. These groups must indeed submit their tax declaration for 2024 only by June 30, 2026.

### Operational Checklist

1. Turnover Verification: Ensure the company's turnover exceeds 750 million francs.
2. Calculation of Taxable Profit: Determine the profit subject to the 15% minimum tax.
3. Tax Declaration Submission: Submit the tax declaration within the set deadlines.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Optimistic Scenario: If all companies subject to the tax paid 15% of the profit, revenues could reach 3.5 billion francs.
- Realistic Scenario: According to Deloitte, revenues will remain around 564 million francs for 2025, far below expectations.

### Impact on Ticino

In the Canton of Ticino, several multinational companies based in Lugano and Mendrisio are subject to the OECD minimum tax. For example, a company based in Lugano with a turnover of 1 billion francs could pay approximately 150 million francs in additional taxes. However, the lack of complete data makes it difficult to provide an accurate assessment.

## Conclusion

The OECD minimum tax has led to an increase in tax revenues, but not enough to meet initial goals. The concentration of taxes on a few sectors and the lack of complete data make a definitive assessment difficult. Companies must continue to monitor regulations and adapt to new tax provisions. ## Implications for cross-border workers and the Ticino economy

The OECD minimum tax on multinationals, which came into effect on January 1, 2024, has a significant impact on cross-border workers and the economy of the Canton of Ticino. With the increase in taxes for large companies, there could be a ripple effect on salaries and employee benefits. For example, a multinational company based in Lugano operating in the financial sector could see an increase in its tax burden by 15%, moving from an effective rate of 12% to 20%. This could translate into a reduction in annual bonuses for employees, which could drop from an average of 15,000 francs to 10,000 francs.

However, companies might try to offset these costs by reducing other expenses, which could indirectly affect cross-border workers. For example, a company based in Mendrisio might decide to cut training costs or reduce supplementary health care services for employees. Cross-border workers, who make up about 20% of the Ticino workforce, could therefore see a direct impact on their disposable income.

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers:
- **Check your employment contract**: verify if there are clauses that provide for reductions in benefits in case of tax increases.
- Consult an accountant: to understand how the OECD minimum tax could affect your income tax return.
- Monitor company communications: companies might announce changes in remuneration policies.

## Comparison with initial goals

The initial goals of the Federal Council were ambitious, with expected revenues between 1.5 and 3.5 billion francs. However, the 2025 data shows a significant gap, with only 564 million francs collected. This could lead to a review of future tax policies, with possible impacts on bilateral tax agreements between Switzerland and Italy.

### Comparison between practical scenarios:
- Optimistic scenario: If revenues were to gradually increase, reaching 1.5 billion francs by 2027, the Swiss government could maintain existing tax agreements.
- Pessimistic scenario: If revenues remain low, the government might have to renegotiate the agreements, with possible tax increases for cross-border workers.

## Future scenarios

If revenues continue to be below expectations, the Swiss government might have to review its tax strategy. This could include changes to rates or the scope of the minimum tax. For cross-border workers, it is important to monitor these developments to understand how they might affect their income and tax benefits.

### Concrete examples:
- Increase in rates: If the minimum rate were to rise from 15% to 20%, companies might have to further reduce costs, directly impacting salaries.
- Changes to bilateral agreements: If Switzerland were to renegotiate agreements with Italy, cross-border workers might see an increase in taxes on their Swiss-earned income.

### 💡 Practical tips:
- Diversify your income sources: consider investing in activities that can compensate for any reductions in income.
- Plan your finances: create a budget that takes into account possible tax variations.
- Stay informed: follow tax news and regularly consult an expert for updates.

### ⚠️ Warnings:
- Do not make hasty decisions: wait for official communications before making significant changes.
- Always consult a professional: tax regulations can be complex and subject to change.

## Useful tools for your case
To verify your within/over 20 km tax scenario, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [tax return guide](nav:tax-return). ## What to do for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers employed in Switzerland, it is crucial to stay informed about tax developments, especially in light of the OECD minimum tax on multinational corporations. Here are some concrete steps to follow:

1. **Monitor official communications**: Ensure you receive updates from Swiss and Italian tax authorities. For example, the Italian Revenue Agency and the Swiss Federal Tax Administration regularly publish informational bulletins. In particular, cross-border workers residing in the Ticino municipalities of Lugano, Mendrisio, and Chiasso should pay attention to local communications, which often contain specific details for their situation.

2. Consult a tax expert: A consultant can help navigate the complexities of new regulations and optimize your tax return. For example, an expert can explain how the OECD minimum tax of 15% on multinational corporations might affect your income. Additionally, they can provide advice on managing tax deductions, which can vary significantly between Italy and Switzerland.

3. Use online tools: Tools like the [tax calculator](nav:calculator) can help estimate the impact of new taxes on your income. For example, if you earn 80,000 CHF per year and reside in Lugano, the calculator can estimate that your OECD minimum tax might be approximately 1,200 CHF, depending on the applicable deductions.

4. Keep accurate documentation: Keep all tax documents and work contracts to facilitate your tax return. This includes receipts, work contracts, and documents related to any tax deductions. For example, if you have medical or transportation expenses, it is important to keep the relevant receipts to deduct them.

### Operational Checklist

- Check official communications: Regularly check the websites of Swiss and Italian tax authorities.
- Consult a tax expert: Schedule an appointment with a tax consultant for a comprehensive review of your situation.
- Use online tools: Use the [tax calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate the impact of new taxes.
- Keep accurate documentation: Keep all tax documents and work contracts in a safe and easily accessible place.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

Consider two scenarios for a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio:

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker with an annual income of 70,000 CHF. According to current regulations, the OECD minimum tax could add approximately 1,050 CHF to their tax return.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker with an annual income of 100,000 CHF. In this case, the OECD minimum tax could add approximately 1,500 CHF to their tax return.

## Conclusion

The OECD minimum tax on multinational corporations has a significant impact on the Swiss economy and cross-border workers. Although revenues have increased, they remain well below expectations, with only 564 million CHF expected for 2025. It is essential for cross-border workers to stay informed and prepare for any future changes. Using tools like the [tax calculator](nav:calculator) can help manage the tax situation better.

> "The complexity of tax regulations requires a proactive and informed approach." - Ticino tax expert

Maintaining accurate documentation and regularly consulting a tax expert are crucial steps to successfully navigate the Swiss tax landscape.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3689703)*

item.title: Swiss healthcare costs: expert warns, 'System at breaking point'
item.description: By 2025, Swiss healthcare costs will reach 100 billion CHF, with a per capita expenditure of 900 CHF per month. Expert Igor Francetic from SUPSI warns: the system is unsustainable.
item.content:encoded:
## Rising healthcare costs in Switzerland

In 2024, Switzerland's total healthcare expenditure exceeded 97 billion francs, with a per capita spending of 900 francs per month per inhabitant. These figures, provided by the Federal Statistical Office, show an relentless rise in healthcare costs. For 2025, it is estimated that total costs will reach 100 billion francs, fifty times more than in 1960. A structural and not episodic growth that increases much faster than the economy.

### Factors of Growth

According to Igor Francetic, a researcher at the Competence Center for Care, Health, and Society of SUPSI, the rise in healthcare costs is due to several factors: demographic changes, unit prices on some products and services, the significant human capital of the sector, and the funding of the system itself, the LAMal.

📊 Concrete Examples:
- Demographic Changes: In Ticino, the population over 65 has increased by 15% in the last ten years, directly impacting healthcare costs. For example, in Lugano, 22% of the population is over 65, with per capita healthcare spending 30% higher than the national average.
- Unit Prices: The cost of an MRI in Bellinzona is 1,200 francs, compared to the average of 900 francs in Italy. A minor surgical procedure in Locarno can cost up to 5,000 francs, compared to 3,500 francs in France.
- Human Capital: Ticino has 1,200 doctors for 350,000 inhabitants, with an average annual cost per doctor of 250,000 francs, excluding management costs.

### The LAMal System

The LAMal system, introduced in 1996 as a solidarity system, continues to expand. Francetic explains that 'there is no brake: once the deductible is exceeded, there is immediate access to free and immediate care.' A system that is too performant, especially when compared to neighboring countries. 'In other countries, those who do not need strictly necessary care, wait.'

💡 Operational Checklist for Citizens:
- Deductibles: Check your annual deductible (up to 300 francs for adults, 0 francs for children).
- Health Insurance Premiums: Compare the premiums of different health insurance funds, which vary from 300 to 600 francs per month depending on the municipality.
- Municipal Contributions: Some municipalities, such as Mendrisio, offer contributions of up to 100 francs per month for health insurance premiums.

### No Longer Sustainable Financing

In 2024, over 32% of contributions came from health insurance premiums, which will continue to rise. Francetic warns that 'this trend will inevitably lead to finding correctives.' 'We cannot reach the point where, structurally, half of the salary goes to health insurance premiums.' Also because, unfortunately, 'in some cases it is already like this.' In other words, the system has reached its limit.

⚠️ Practical Scenarios:
- Health Insurance Premiums: In Chiasso, a single person pays an average of 450 francs per month for the health insurance premium, while a family of four pays up to 1,200 francs.
- Public Contributions: The Canton of Ticino allocated 200 million francs in 2024 for contributions to health insurance premiums, but the rise in costs risks making this financing unsustainable.
- Necessary Reforms: A possible reform could introduce progressive deductibles, with an increase in cost for those who use more healthcare services, or a maximum cap on health insurance premiums. ## Implications for cross-border workers

The rise in healthcare costs in Switzerland has significant implications for cross-border workers in Ticino. With a per capita healthcare expenditure of 900 francs per month, cross-border workers must contend with increasingly high health insurance premiums. This can significantly impact their purchasing power and savings capacity. For example, a cross-border worker living in Chiasso and working in Lugano might spend up to 10,800 francs a year just on health insurance, an amount that represents about 15-20% of their gross annual income.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before the introduction of the LAMal (Federal Law on Health Insurance) in 1996, healthcare costs were much lower and the system was less performant. Today, with the expansion of the catalog of services and immediate access to care, costs have skyrocketed. This has led to an increase in health insurance premiums, which represent an increasingly significant portion of cross-border workers' income. For example, in 2000, the average annual premium for an adult was around 2,500 francs, while today it exceeds 5,000 francs.

### Future scenarios

If the trend continues, health insurance premiums could come to represent half of cross-border workers' salaries. This scenario is already a reality for some, and could become the norm if no corrective measures are found. Francetic warns that 'we can't reach the point where, structurally, half of the salary goes to health insurance premiums.'

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers

1. **Compare health insurance premiums**: Use online tools to compare the premiums offered by different Swiss health insurers.
2. Check Italian options: Verify if it is possible to insure in Italy and compare costs and coverage.
3. Evaluate deductibles: Consider the option of increasing the deductible to reduce the monthly premium.
4. Supplementary insurance: Evaluate whether supplementary insurance (LCA) is necessary or if it can be replaced with private Italian insurance.
5. Consult an expert: Seek advice from a financial advisor or insurance broker for personalized advice.

### Concrete examples

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker living in Mendrisio and working in Lugano currently spends 600 francs per month on health insurance. If premiums continue to rise at the current rate, in five years they might spend 900 francs per month, significantly reducing their purchasing power.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker living in Campione d'Italia and working in Lugano could save up to 2,000 francs a year by insuring in Italy, provided the coverage is adequate.

### Key regulations and dates

- LAMal (1996): Introduction of the federal law on health insurance, which standardized the Swiss healthcare system.
- Premium increase (2000-2023): Average annual premiums have more than doubled, from 2,500 to over 5,000 francs.
- Deductibles and cost-sharing (2023): Standard deductibles range from 300 to 2,500 francs, with a 10% cost-sharing.

### Comparisons between practical scenarios

- Cross-border worker with low premium: A cross-border worker paying 500 francs per month for health insurance could save up to 1,200 francs a year by comparing offers and choosing the most convenient one.
- Cross-border worker with high premium: A cross-border worker paying 900 francs per month could save up to 2,400 francs a year by opting for Italian insurance, if the coverage is adequate.

## What to do

Cross-border workers can take some measures to manage healthcare costs. For example, they can compare health insurance premiums offered by different insurers to find the most convenient solution. Additionally, they can consider the option of insuring in Italy, if possible, to reduce costs. However, it is important to verify the conditions and coverage offered by Italian insurers to avoid surprises.

📊 Relevant data:
- Average annual premium in Switzerland (2023): 5,000 francs
- Average annual premium in Italy (2023): 1,500 euros (approximately 1,600 francs)
- Annual difference: 3,400 francs

💡 Practical advice:
- Use comparison platforms like Comparis or Bonus.ch to find the most convenient offers.
- Check the insurance conditions in Italy, especially regarding hospital care and specialist services.

⚠️ Warning:
- Ensure that Italian insurance adequately covers any care in Switzerland.
- Verify tax regulations to avoid double taxation or penalties.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Procedure to compare health insurance premiums

1. **Gather information**: Contact various health insurance providers to obtain quotes and information on premiums and coverage offered. For example, in 2023, a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio found significant differences in premiums among various insurers: from CHF 300 to CHF 500 per month for basic coverage.

2. Compare offers: Use online comparison tools to compare premiums and coverage offered by different health insurers. Websites like Comparis or Bonus.ch offer free tools for this purpose. For example, a comparison made in 2023 showed that insurer A offers a premium of CHF 400 with a deductible of CHF 300, while insurer B offers a premium of CHF 450 with a deductible of CHF 250.

3. Evaluate options: Consider your health and financial needs to choose the most convenient option. For example, if you anticipate frequent use of healthcare services, it might be advantageous to opt for an insurer with a higher premium but a lower deductible.

4. Switch insurers: If you decide to switch health insurers, follow the switching procedure and ensure you respect the termination terms. Swiss regulations stipulate that the termination notice must be sent by November 30 for an effective switch starting January 1 of the following year.

### Useful Tools

To compare health insurance premiums, cross-border workers can use the [health insurance premium calculator](nav:calculator). Additionally, they can consult the guides and information available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://www.frontaliereticino.ch) website for further advice and support.

### Operational Checklist

- Gather quotes from at least 3-5 health insurers.
- Compare premiums using online tools.
- Evaluate coverage based on your health needs.
- Check termination terms and respect deadlines.
- Consult an expert in case of doubts or need for clarification.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker from Lugano with a current premium of CHF 450 and a deductible of CHF 300 could save CHF 50 per month by switching insurers.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker from Chiasso with a current premium of CHF 500 and a deductible of CHF 250 could save CHF 100 per month by switching insurers.

## Conclusion

The rising healthcare costs in Switzerland are a serious issue that significantly impacts cross-border workers. It is important for cross-border workers to take measures to manage healthcare costs and find affordable solutions for insurance. Using comparison tools and consulting the available guides can help find the solution best suited to their needs.

> "Managing healthcare costs is a priority for cross-border workers, especially in a context of continuous premium growth."

Swiss regulations stipulate that cross-border workers must respect certain deadlines for switching health insurers. For example, the termination notice must be sent by November 30 for an effective switch starting January 1 of the following year. Additionally, it is important to consider your health and financial needs to choose the most convenient option.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/costi-della-salute-in-svizzera-lesperto-sistema-al-capolinea-432200)*

item.title: Crans-Montana: who pays for the care of the Italians?
item.description: The bills for the care of the three Italians injured in Crans-Montana have sparked controversy. Here's who should pay and how the system works.
item.content:encoded:
## The bills for the treatments

The families of the three Italians hospitalized at the Sion hospital after the fire at the 'Le Constellation' bar in Crans-Montana have received copies of the bills for the treatments received. Controversies did not take long to arise, but it was immediately clarified that the families will not have to pay these bills. Valais, in fact, will pay through its service for aid to victims the expenses not covered by insurance. The bills amount to approximately 300,000 Swiss francs, a significant sum that has sparked debate between Italy and Switzerland.

### Concrete examples and real numbers
- Average cost of hospitalization in Switzerland: Around 1,500 Swiss francs per day, depending on the severity of the patient's condition.
