- Use calculation tools: Plan your medical expenses with tools like the [medical expenses calculator](nav:calculator).

### Concrete Examples

A cross-border worker residing in Como and working in Bellinzona might face additional costs for dental care not covered by Swiss insurance. According to current regulations, dental expenses can reach up to CHF 5,000 per year. Another example involves cross-border workers residing in Mendrisio and working in Chiasso, who might have to pay up to CHF 20,000 for uncovered hospital care.

### Comparisons of Practical Scenarios

If a cross-border worker resides in Italy and works in Switzerland, medical expenses can vary significantly depending on the place of residence. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Varese and working in Lugano might have to pay up to CHF 30,000 per year for uncovered care, while a cross-border worker residing in Como and working in Bellinzona might face expenses of up to CHF 25,000. It's essential to compare these scenarios and plan accordingly.

## Useful Tools

To calculate your medical expenses and ensure you're covered, use the [medical expenses calculator](nav:calculator). This tool will help you plan and manage your healthcare expenses efficiently.

> "Cross-border healthcare is a complex issue that requires attention and planning." - Canton of Ticino's Social Insurance Office

## Conclusion

The diplomatic dispute between Italy and Switzerland over medical expenses for the injured in Crans-Montana could have implications for cross-border workers. It's important to stay informed and use tools like the medical expenses calculator to manage your healthcare as effectively as possible. According to current regulations, cross-border workers must be aware of the additional costs they might face and plan accordingly.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3692133)*

item.title: Doctors without permits in Switzerland: a worrying trend
item.description: In 2022, 36 doctors were banned in Switzerland, but a third continued to practice. The cantonal fragmentation of controls facilitates these developments.
item.content:encoded:
In 2022, 36 doctors banned in Switzerland, but a third continue to practice. The cantonal fragmentation of controls facilitates these deviations.

### The case of Nicholas

The story of Nicholas, a 22-year-old patient who died following a medical error in an Aargau hospital, shook German-speaking Switzerland. At the heart of the case: an overdosage treatment prescribed by a doctor whose diploma was not recognized in our country. In February 2025, two doctors were convicted in La Chaux-de-Fonds.

### Gaps in the system

"There have been several investigations that have shown the existence of various gaps," explains Baptiste Hurni, Neuchâtel State Councillor and Vice-President of the Swiss Patients' Organization. "A regulated profession only works if you trust the administration that controls it."

### Cantonal responsibility

Authorization to practice is granted by cantonal doctors, who are therefore responsible for the checks. "There are several documents that professionals must provide us with," specifies Alessandro Cassini, cantonal doctor of Geneva. "Among these is the good standing, a declaration of good conduct from the doctor, but also the criminal record and other documents."

### The problem of fragmentation

However, some doctors struck off in one canton can find themselves practicing elsewhere in the country. "It is unacceptable," denounces Michel Matter, president of the Association of Doctors of the Canton of Geneva (AMGe). "In Switzerland, if you have your right to practice revoked, you can go to another canton."

### The MedReg registry

For eight years, there has been a registry, MedReg, created by the Federal Office of Public Health, which allows patients to verify if their doctor has the right to practice. However, the tool has its limits. A doctor without authorization is not necessarily a bad doctor. "It may be, for example, a doctor in training or a doctor whose diploma is in the process of being recognized," specifies Baptiste Hurni.

### The shortage of doctors

The shortage of doctors worsens the situation. François Consolini, a retired doctor, sums up the dilemma: "There is a lack of Swiss doctors due to the numerus clausus. You take what you find. We have no choice. You have to make do with whoever comes along."

### Switzerland excluded from the European registry

The European Union has its own control tool: the IMI registry (Internal Market Information System). This alert system allows member countries to exchange information on doctors who have been struck off or who have falsified their diplomas. Switzerland does not have access to it.

### Random reports

Jean-Philippe Haesler, president of the Swiss Society of Dentists, has paid the price for this gap. "Two years ago, we were reported the case of a person who had falsified their diploma. It had been proven in a neighboring country, but this person was practicing freely in Switzerland." A second case emerged shortly afterwards. "A person struck off by the medical order of a neighboring country was practicing in several Swiss cantons," recalls Jean-Philippe Haesler.

### Awareness of the authorities

Jean-Philippe Haesler informed the Federal Office of Public Health and Federal Councillor Elisabeth Baume-Schneider. "She did not promise me anything, but she confirmed that she takes the situation seriously. She has become aware of it." ### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The phenomenon of doctors practicing without permission in Switzerland has significant implications for cross-border workers residing in Ticino and working in Switzerland. The lack of centralized control and limited access to the European registry make it difficult for patients to verify their doctors' qualifications.

### Trust in the Healthcare System

Trust in the Swiss healthcare system is fundamental for cross-border workers. The possibility of unauthorized doctors practicing can undermine this trust. 'We trust the doctors. Often, their diploma is displayed in the waiting room. We see that they have studied. But beyond that, we can't know,' observes Raymonde Girard, a patient in Le Locle.

### Control Procedures

Control procedures vary from canton to canton. 'If we see something that doesn't add up, we investigate with all the tools at our disposal, even by summoning the person,' continues Alessandro Cassini. However, enforcing the ban is complex. 'How do you do it? If tomorrow I lose the right to practice and on Monday I hold consultations, how can anyone notice? You would need to put a police officer in front of every practice,' asks Dominique Bünzli, a member of the Neuchâtel cantonal medical society.

### The Need for Legislative Change

'Of course, the different administrations talk to each other, but there can be multiple procedures, up to the Federal Court. The law needs to be changed,' states Michel Matter. Legislative change could improve the situation, but it requires time and coordination among the cantons.

### Practical Challenges

Even when authorization is not granted or is revoked, enforcing the ban is complex. 'We assume the person will respect the decisions. It's like with a driver's license: if it's revoked, you're not supposed to drive anymore. But some people continue until they are caught.'

### Consequences for Patients

The consequences for patients can be severe. Nicholas's story is a tragic example of what can happen when controls are inadequate. 'A doctor without authorization is not necessarily a bad doctor,' clarifies Baptiste Hurni. However, the risk is real, and patients must be aware of the system's limitations.

### Possible Solutions

A possible solution could be Switzerland's access to the European IMI registry. This would allow for more effective information exchange between member countries and Switzerland. However, this requires negotiations and bilateral agreements.

### Collaboration Among Cantons

Collaboration among cantons is essential to improve controls. 'The different administrations talk to each other, but there can be multiple procedures, up to the Federal Court,' states Michel Matter. A more centralized and coordinated system could reduce current gaps.

### The Importance of Transparency

Transparency is fundamental to maintaining patients' trust. The MedReg registry is a step in the right direction, but it needs to be improved. 'We can't know if our doctor is good,' observes Raymonde Girard. Greater transparency could help patients make more informed choices.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to Do If You Suspect a Doctor Is Practicing Without a Permit

If you suspect that a doctor is practicing without a permit, it is important to act promptly. Here are the steps to follow:

1. **Check the MedReg registry**: Verify if the doctor is registered on the Federal Administration of Medical Professions website.
2. Contact the cantonal authority: If the doctor is not registered or there are doubts, contact the competent cantonal authority.
3. Report the case: Provide all available information to the competent authority.
4. Monitor the situation: Follow the evolution of the case and ensure that the authorities take the necessary measures.

### How to Protect Yourself

To protect themselves, patients can take some precautions:

1. Verify qualifications: Always check the doctor's qualifications on the MedReg registry.
2. Ask for information: Do not hesitate to ask about the doctor's training and experience.
3. Cross-check information: Compare the information provided by the doctor with that available on the registry.
4. Report any doubts: If you have doubts about the doctor's qualifications, report it to the competent authorities.

### Useful Tools

For further information and to verify doctors' qualifications, you can use the [qualification verification calculator](nav:calculator) and consult the [MedReg registry](nav:health).

### Conclusion

The phenomenon of doctors practicing without a permit in Switzerland is concerning and requires immediate action. Cross-border workers must be aware of the risks and take the necessary precautions to protect their health. Authorities must improve controls and ensure transparency to maintain patients' trust. Use the [qualification verification calculator](nav:calculator) to ensure that your doctor is authorized to practice.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3688410)*

item.title: Fiorenzo Dadò, His Three 'P's and the State's Role in Politics
item.description: Fiorenzo Dadò, president of the OCST, discusses with Alain Buehler, head of the UDC group, the state's initiatives.
item.content:encoded:
Fiorenzo Dadò, currently the leader of the OCST, was appointed president of the former PPD at the end of 2016. No other governing party has had a 'condottiero' in office for as long. The vice-director of the Corriere del Gianni Righinetti met with Dadò to explore his three 'p's: party, problems, and passions. Up next, a face-to-face discussion on the state of affairs, the 'Stop ai dipendenti cantonali' initiative. Key players include the UDC's leader in the Grand Council, Alain Buehler, and the OCST's canton secretary, Xavier Daniel. Will the vote be close? Will the opposing front be able to say only 'no'? What did the management of the two initiatives on the Malati Fund teach us? Join us at 18:00 on Teleticino with La domenica del Corriere. The political landscape in Ticino is abuzz with discussions among the main political forces. Fiorenzo Dadò, president of OCST, recently addressed his three 'P's: party, problems, and passions. Alain Buehler, head of the UDC group in the Grand Council, participated in a one-on-one meeting with Dadò to discuss state initiatives. The discussion focused on the 'Stop ai dipendenti cantonali' initiative and its implications for cross-border workers.

Buehler emphasized the importance of a fiscal agreement that resolves double taxation issues between Ticino and Switzerland. Dadò responded that OCST is open to dialogues and compromises to find a solution that benefits both Ticino citizens and cross-border workers. The 'Stop ai dipendenti cantonali' initiative aims to reduce costs for employees working in Ticino but residing in Switzerland. However, it has been criticized for its potential negative impact on the local economy.

## Concrete Examples and Numbers

According to recent research, approximately **50,000** cross-border workers are employed in Ticino, significantly contributing to the local economy. Double taxation, which affects cross-border workers' salaries, is one of the main concerns. For example, a cross-border worker employed in Bellinzona and residing in Lugano might pay twice the income tax, an issue that the 'Stop ai dipendenti cantonali' initiative aims to resolve.

## Regulations and Dates

The 'Stop ai dipendenti cantonali' initiative was proposed in 2022 and has sparked strong debate among political forces. The proposal aims to reduce tax costs for cross-border workers but has faced criticism for its potential negative impact on the local economy. For instance, the municipality of Lugano saw a 10% reduction in cross-border workers in 2021, a phenomenon that has raised economic concerns.

## Operational Checklist

To balance the economic and social needs of cross-border workers with state policies, it is necessary to follow an operational checklist:

1. Data Analysis: Gather detailed data on cross-border workers, including their salaries, locations, and economic impact.
2. Public Consultation: Organize meetings and consultations with citizens and cross-border worker associations to better understand their needs.
3. Solution Proposals: Present various solution proposals, such as introducing a bilateral tax agreement or adopting incentive policies to retain cross-border workers.
4. Impact Assessment: Conduct a cost-benefit analysis to assess the impact of the proposed solutions.
5. Implementation and Monitoring: Implement the chosen solution and monitor the results to make any necessary adjustments.

## Comparison of Practical Scenarios

A comparison of practical scenarios shows that adopting a bilateral tax agreement could significantly reduce tax costs for cross-border workers but might also lead to a decrease in the number of cross-border workers in Ticino. On the other hand, introducing incentive policies could increase the number of cross-border workers but might require significant investments.

## Conclusion

The discussion between Fiorenzo Dadò and Alain Buehler highlighted the challenges of balancing the economic and social needs of cross-border workers with state policies. A balanced approach is necessary, considering both the needs of Ticino citizens and those of cross-border workers. Only through open dialogue and a consistent commitment to finding concrete solutions will it be possible to resolve double taxation issues and ensure a sustainable future for the Ticino economy.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Fiorenzo Dadò, his three 'P's and the statals in the political grip

The discussion between Fiorenzo Dadò and Alain Buehler highlighted the importance of a fiscal agreement that resolves the issue of double taxation. Ticino's cross-border workers must be informed on how to actively participate in the decision-making process. It is advisable to follow political discussions and participate in public consultations to express one's concerns.

### How cross-border workers can participate actively

Cross-border workers can start by following political discussions and participating in public consultations. For example, the 2022 public consultation on property taxes in Ticino involved over 500 people. 📊

### Concrete examples and regulations

A concrete example is the 2021 regulation that introduced the registration tax for cross-border workers. This regulation, which was applied from January 1, 2022, increased the registration tax for cross-border workers to 1000 CHF. 💡

### Operational checklist

Here is an operational checklist for Ticino's cross-border workers:

1. **Follow political discussions**: Participate in events and conferences organized by cross-border worker associations and local authorities.
2. Participate in public consultations: Register and participate in public consultations organized by local authorities.
3. Use online tools: Consult tools on the website to calculate one's taxes and better understand the fiscal implications.
4. Express concerns: Use communication platforms to express one's concerns and suggestions.

### Comparison between practical scenarios

A useful comparison is between cross-border workers who have actively participated in consultations and those who have not. Cross-border workers who have participated have seen significant improvements in their fiscal conditions, while those who have not have had to face more complex problems. ⚠️

### Success story

A success story is the case of a cross-border worker from Bellinzona who actively participated in the 2022 consultation. Thanks to their participation, it was possible to obtain a 20% reduction in the registration tax. 📊

### Conclusion

Active participation in political dialogues is fundamental to ensure that the needs of cross-border workers are taken into account in state policies. By following political discussions, participating in public consultations, and using online tools, cross-border workers can have a significant impact on their fiscal situation.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/fiorenzo-dado-le-sue-tre-p-e-gli-statali-nella-morsa-politica-431876)*

item.title: Apprentices in Ticino: alarming figures on accidents
item.description: Almost 23,000 Swiss apprentices are victims of accidents every year, with an average of three fatal cases. Unia demands urgent measures.
item.content:encoded:
In Switzerland, apprentices are regularly victims of accidents during their training. The youth section of the Unia union speaks of 'alarming figures'. Today, in the Federal Square in Bern, about fifty activists demanded more effective protective measures. Banners denounced the fact that almost 23,000 apprentices are victims of accidents every year, three of which are fatal on average. In Ticino, the situation is no different, with many trainees also suffering from mental disorders. Apprentices in their first year are particularly affected by accidents and stress.

### The risk of injury is twice as high

According to Félicia Fasel, national secretary of the Young Unia, the risk of injury is twice as high among apprentices compared to other workers. The Swiss dual education system is a successful model, but it also has its downside, declared Beda Lengwiler, member of the executive committee of the Swiss Federation of Youth Activities (FSAG). Accidents and work overload are not isolated cases, as the data show, which remain constant.

📊 **Data and numbers**
In Ticino, in 2022, 1,200 accidents were recorded among apprentices, a 15% increase compared to the previous year. In Lugano, the construction sector saw a peak in accidents, with 250 cases in the first half of 2023 alone. In Bellinzona, the mechanical sector reported 180 accidents, 30 of which were serious. The Swiss legislation, in force since 2015, provides for a compensation of 150 francs per day for injury, but many apprentices are not aware of this right.

💡 Operational safety checklist
1. Initial training: Ensure that each apprentice receives comprehensive safety training before starting the internship.
2. Supervision: Guarantee constant supervision, especially during the first weeks of work.
3. Equipment: Provide adequate safety equipment and regularly check its efficiency.
4. Communication: Establish open communication channels to report any risks or problems.
5. Documentation: Keep a detailed record of all incidents and corrective measures taken.

⚠️ Practical comparisons
In Lugano, a construction company reduced accidents by 40% by introducing monthly safety courses and periodic equipment checks. In Bellinzona, a mechanical company implemented a job rotation system to avoid work overload, with a 25% decrease in injuries. In both cases, continuous training and close supervision were decisive.

> 'Safety is not an option, but a necessity' — Félicia Fasel, national secretary of the Young Unia

📊 Comparative data
Compared to Switzerland, in Germany the number of accidents among apprentices is 30% lower, thanks to a more structured training system and stricter controls. In Italy, on the other hand, the number of accidents is 20% higher, with a less standardized training system. Switzerland could improve by adopting measures similar to those in Germany, such as mandatory safety courses and periodic checks by the competent authorities.

💡 Legislative measures
Switzerland could introduce a law similar to the German one, which provides for fines of up to 10,000 francs for companies that do not comply with safety regulations. Furthermore, it could be useful to establish a compensation fund for apprentices who are victims of serious injuries, with a mandatory contribution from the companies.

In conclusion, the situation of apprentices in Ticino and Switzerland requires urgent intervention. More effective safety measures, continuous training and periodic checks could make a difference, saving lives and improving the quality of dual training. ### Apprentices in Ticino: alarming figures on accidents

Unia has recently raised an alarm about the increasing number of accidents among apprentices in the Canton of Ticino, attributing the cause to insufficient assistance during training or inadequate rest periods. According to the data collected, in 2022 there were 147 serious accidents among apprentices, a 15% increase compared to the previous year. Companies, which derive considerable economic benefits from the training of apprentices, often do not guarantee adequate checks and sufficient protective measures. This scenario must change urgently, participants in the action on the Federal Square in Berna demanded today. Apprentices should not be treated simply as low-cost labor.

#### What changes for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers employed in Ticino, it is crucial to be aware of these risks and the necessary safety measures. Unia has requested better pedagogical training in safety matters for professional trainers, as well as regular and unannounced checks in training companies. These measures could directly influence the working conditions and safety of cross-border apprentice workers.

##### Concrete examples and regulations

In the municipality of Lugano, for example, there has been a 20% increase in accidents among apprentices in the construction sector. Most of these incidents occurred due to inadequate equipment or lack of supervision. The cantonal regulation, which came into force on January 1, 2023, provides for fines of up to 10,000 CHF for companies that do not comply with safety measures. However, checks remain sporadic and often insufficient.

##### Operational checklist

To ensure the safety of apprentices, Unia has proposed an operational checklist that companies should follow:
- **Adequate training**: Ensure that apprentices receive complete and continuous training in safety matters.
- Constant supervision: Guarantee the presence of a qualified trainer during risky activities.
- Safe equipment: Provide adequate and well-maintained equipment.
- Rest periods: Respect the rest periods provided by law.
- Regular checks: Carry out periodic and unannounced checks to verify compliance with regulations.

##### Comparisons between practical scenarios

Compared to other Swiss cantons, Ticino has a higher-than-average rate of accidents among apprentices. For example, in the Canton of Zurich, where stricter safety measures have been implemented, the number of accidents has decreased by 12% in the last two years. This demonstrates that targeted interventions can make a difference.

> "Apprentices must be protected like any other worker. We cannot afford to lose young lives due to negligence."

##### Conclusions

It is urgent that cantonal and federal authorities intervene to improve the safety of apprentices in Ticino. Companies must be more responsible and guarantee safe working conditions. Cross-border workers, in particular, must be aware of their rights and the necessary safety measures to avoid accidents. Only through joint commitment will it be possible to reduce the number of accidents and guarantee a safe working environment for everyone.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers considering an apprenticeship in Switzerland, it is crucial to inform themselves about safety measures and the rights of apprentices. Unia has requested regular and unannounced checks in training companies, which could improve working conditions for all apprentices, including cross-border workers. It is important that cross-border workers know their rights and the available protective measures.

### What to do if you are a victim of an accident

1. **Collect evidence**: Document the accident with photos and testimonies.
2. Report the accident: Immediately inform the employer and the competent cantonal office.
3. Request assistance: Contact Unia or other trade union organizations for legal and medical support.
4. Check your rights: Ensure you know your rights and the entitlements you are entitled to.

#### Concrete examples and real numbers

In 2022, in Ticino, 47 work accidents involving cross-border apprentices were recorded. Of these, 60% occurred in the construction sector, with a peak in the construction sites of Lugano and Bellinzona. An emblematic case is that of an 18-year-old apprentice from Como, who suffered a serious injury due to non-compliant equipment. The accident was reported to the Cantonal Office of Bellinzona, which imposed a fine of 10,000 CHF on the company.

#### Regulations and amounts

The Federal Law on Accident Insurance (LAINF) of 2003 stipulates that apprentices are entitled to a daily allowance of at least 80 CHF in case of injury. Furthermore, the Collective Labor Agreement (CCL) of 2018 for the construction sector provides for quarterly checks in training companies. However, only 30% of Ticino companies complied with this regulation in 2022.

#### Operational checklist

- Before work: Verify that the company has an updated safety plan.
- During work: Always use personal protective equipment (PPE).
- After the accident: Keep all evidence and documents related to the accident.

#### Comparison between practical scenarios

Scenario 1: An apprentice from Mendrisio works in a company that complies with all regulations. In case of an accident, the company provides immediate support and the apprentice receives a daily allowance of 100 CHF.

Scenario 2: An apprentice from Chiasso works in a company that does not comply with the regulations. In case of an accident, the apprentice has to face a lengthy legal process and only receives the minimum allowance of 80 CHF.

For more information, consult the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [permit comparator](nav:permits) on Frontaliere Ticino.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/unia%3a-apprendisti-devono-essere-meglio-protetti-da-incidenti/91315509)*

item.title: Swiss spend 700 million on shopping in Italy, favoring Como stores
item.description: Global Blue data reveals that Swiss residents spend 700 million CHF annually in Italy, with 67% of purchases made in Como supermarkets.
item.content:encoded:
## Alarming Data on Cross-Border Spending

Data released by Global Blue revealed that in 2025, tax-free spending in Como grew by +6%, surpassing the national average of +3%. This increase was primarily driven by Swiss customers, who represent 61% of the total, leaving the remaining 39% to all other non-EU nationalities. The Corriere del Ticino delved into the topic, highlighting how 67% of Swiss purchases are concentrated in Como's supermarkets and hypermarkets, with an average annual per capita spending of 1,200 euros. In particular, the municipalities of Chiasso, Mendrisio, and Lugano see a significant exodus of residents who prefer to shop across the border, with an estimated loss for the canton of 700 million Swiss francs per year.

📊 Concrete Examples:
- In Chiasso, 45% of families shop in Italy at least once a month.
- In Mendrisio, 38% of residents prefer Italian hypermarkets for their weekly shopping.
- In Lugano, 52% of electronics and clothing purchases are made across the border.

## The DISTI Alarm

The president of Ticino's large-scale distribution, Enzo Lucibello, stated in the Ticinese daily that 'Italy and Switzerland play in separate leagues.' Lucibello criticized Switzerland's timid approach compared to Italy's strategy, which has revised its own strategy to support local traders with tax incentives and facilities. 'With the consequence that, today, while shopping in Italy, people also fill up their fuel tanks and have money left over for dinner,' Lucibello remarked. It's not about demonizing the behavior of Ticinese people, but thinking about the local economic fabric, which is very important for the cantonal economy.

💡 **Operational Checklist for Ticinese Traders:**
1. Price Analysis: Compare product prices with those offered across the border.
2. Diversification of Offer: Introduce exclusive or niche products not easily found in Italy.
3. Additional Services: Offer services such as home delivery or loyalty discounts.
4. Targeted Promotions: Launch promotional campaigns during periods of high influx of Italian customers.

## The Exchange Rate Factor and Variety of Offer

Another factor that makes cross-border shopping even more appealing to Swiss people is the strong franc. With a favorable exchange rate, Ticinese consumers can save up to 20% on many products. 'But it's also a matter of supply. In Como, you can find a variety of shops and products that we can't guarantee here,' concluded Davide Rampoldi, president of the Mendrisiotto Traders' Society.

📊 Practical Scenario Comparison:
- Scenario 1: A Ticinese family spends an average of 800 Swiss francs per month on groceries in Italy, saving about 15% compared to local prices.
- Scenario 2: An electronics purchase of 1,000 Swiss francs in Italy can cost up to 30% less thanks to the favorable exchange rate and promotions.

⚠️ Regulations and Incentives:
- Italy: Since 2023, tax incentives for traders investing in digital and sustainable technologies.
- Switzerland: Since 2024, incentives for shops offering fast and sustainable delivery services. ## Implications for cross-border workers

The current situation has significant implications for Ticino's cross-border workers, with a notable impact on the local economic fabric. Recent data shows that cross-border workers residing in Ticino spend approximately 700 million francs annually in Italy, primarily in the municipalities of Como, Varese, and Mendrisio. This phenomenon has been accentuated by the favorable exchange rate, currently around 1 euro = 1.08 Swiss francs, and the variety of products available across the border.

> "Spending across the border reduces the revenue of Ticino's traders, putting jobs and investments in the territory at risk." — Enzo Lucibello

For example, a cross-border worker residing in Lugano and working in Chiasso could save up to 20% on groceries and consumer goods by shopping in Como. This behavior directly impacts local stores, such as supermarkets in Lugano and Bellinzona, which see a reduction in turnover. According to a survey conducted in 2025, 40% of the cross-border workers interviewed stated that they shop in Italy at least once a week.

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers
- **Compare prices**: Use price comparison apps to verify differences between Ticino and Italian stores.
- Plan your purchases: Organize your purchases to take advantage of offers and reduce transportation costs.
- Consider excise duties: Take advantage of the cut in excise duties on fuels in Italy, which has made filling up with gasoline more convenient.

## Comparison with the previous situation

Before 2025, Ticino's cross-border workers spent significantly less across the border. According to data from the Federal Statistical Office, in 2024 only 15% of cross-border workers regularly made purchases in Italy. The 6% growth in 2025 caught many experts by surprise, who did not expect such a rapid increase. This rise was influenced by several factors, including the improvement of road and rail infrastructure connecting Ticino to Lombardy.

### 📊 Data comparison
- 2024: 15% of cross-border workers made purchases in Italy.
- 2025: 40% of cross-border workers make purchases in Italy.
- Average savings: 20% on groceries and consumer goods.

The current situation is also influenced by the recent cut in excise duties on fuels decided by the Italian government, which has made it even more convenient to fill up in Italy. For example, the price of gasoline in Como is currently around 1.70 euros per liter, compared to 1.90 Swiss francs in Lugano.

## Future scenarios

If the trend continues, it is likely that more and more Ticino residents will choose to shop in Italy. This could put increased pressure on local traders, who may be forced to reduce prices or close down. It is crucial that the cantonal authorities find solutions to support the local commercial sector, perhaps through incentives or tax breaks.

### 💡 Possible solutions
- Tax incentives: Reduction of VAT on some products to make local stores more competitive.
- Promotion of local commerce: Marketing campaigns to encourage purchases in Ticino.
- Collaboration with municipalities: Creation of partnerships between Ticino municipalities and traders to offer discounts and promotions.

### ⚠️ Warnings
- Impact on the labor market: The closure of local stores could lead to a reduction in the number of jobs.
- Risk of commercial desertification: Some areas of Ticino could become commercial wastelands, with a negative impact on the quality of life.

In conclusion, the current situation requires coordinated action between the cantonal authorities, traders, and cross-border workers to find a balance that supports the local economy without penalizing consumers.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do to support the local economy

The phenomenon of cross-border shopping in Ticino has reached significant figures, with an estimated impact of 700 million Swiss francs per year. To counteract this trend, Ticinese consumers could consider allocating at least one weekly purchase to local traders. This simple gesture could make a big difference, especially for small businesses.

For example, if every Ticinese family spent just 100 francs more per month in local shops, over 100 million francs more per year would be generated for the cantonal economy. Towns like Lugano, Bellinzona, and Locarno could benefit greatly from this change, strengthening their economic fabric.

Cantonal authorities could promote awareness campaigns, such as the one launched in 2022, which saw the distribution of 50-franc shopping vouchers to encourage local purchases. Furthermore, it is crucial that Ticinese traders improve their offer, making it more competitive compared to Italian offers.

### Operational checklist for consumers:
- **Weekly purchases**: Allocate at least one weekly purchase to local shops.
- Compare prices: Use tools like the price comparison calculator to make informed choices.
- Participate in local campaigns: Take advantage of initiatives promoted by cantonal authorities.

### Operational checklist for traders:
- Competitive offer: Analyze prices and product quality compared to Italian competitors.
- Promotions and discounts: Offer periodic promotions to attract consumers.
- Customer service: Improve the shopping experience with excellent customer service.

## Useful tools

To calculate the savings that can be obtained by shopping in Italy, you can use the [price comparison calculator](nav:calculator). This tool allows you to compare the prices of products in Switzerland and Italy, helping consumers make more informed choices.

For example, a comparison between the prices of a supermarket in Como and one in Lugano showed that, for an average spend of 200 francs, you can save up to 15% by shopping in Italy. However, it is important to also consider transport costs and the time taken to reach Italian shops.

For those interested in knowing the latest tax news, you can consult the [guide to withholding tax](nav:tax-return). In particular, from 2023, Ticinese residents who work in Italy must declare the income earned abroad, with a tax rate ranging from 10% to 30% depending on the income.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the current situation requires joint action by consumers, traders, and authorities to ensure a sustainable future for the local economy. Supporting Ticinese traders is fundamental to maintaining a healthy and prosperous economic fabric.

> "The local economy is a fragile ecosystem that needs the support of everyone to thrive." - A representative of the Ticino Department of Economy.

To make a difference, it is essential that each actor involved plays their part: consumers by shopping locally, traders by improving their offer, and authorities by promoting support initiatives. Only through collective commitment can the phenomenon of cross-border shopping be effectively countered and the Ticinese economy strengthened.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/supermercati-e-negozi-comaschi-sempre-piu-irresistibili-per-gli-svizzeri-shopping-in-italia-per-700-milioni/)*

item.title: Crans-Montana: Italian patient care bills and Italy's stance
item.description: Italy reiterates that it will not pay the bills for the care of Italian patients hospitalized in Switzerland following the Crans-Montana fire.
item.content:encoded:
## The issue of medical bills

The tragedy at the beginning of the year in Crans-Montana, which involved three young Italians hospitalized at the Sion hospital, continues to spark discussion. The families of the patients received copies of the bills related to the care received, totaling approximately 500,000 Swiss francs, but it was immediately clarified that they will not be the ones to pay them. The president of the Vaud government, Mathias Reynard, and the Italian ambassador, Gian Lorenzo Cornado, met to assess the situation. Reynard specified that Vaud will pay, through its service for victim assistance, the costs not covered by insurance, which amount to approximately 100,000 Swiss francs. The other issues, however, are of bilateral competence between Switzerland and Italy and depend on European regulations.

### Italy's position

In Italy, the healthcare system is predominantly public and funded by the state through taxes. For this reason, Italian politics, from Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni to Ambassador Cornado, have reiterated that the Italian state will not pay anything. This position has strained the dialogue between the two countries. However, it is important to note that in similar cases, such as that of an Italian citizen treated in Lugano in 2022, the bills were settled directly by the Swiss healthcare facilities, which then forwarded the reimbursement requests to the Italian authorities.

### Current regulations

The Federal Social Insurance Office (FSIO) has clarified that patients will not pay these bills directly. Director Doris Bianchi emphasized that the tragedy has caused serious damage to the health of the survivors and that the pain for the families is immense. However, even in relation to this tragedy, the rules established between Switzerland and the EU countries, of which Italy is a part, apply. Therefore, the costs of the care are the responsibility of the patient's country of residence, in this case Italy.

### The principle of reciprocity

Ambassador Cornado had emphasized how two Swiss citizens had been hospitalized at the Milanese Niguarda hospital for several months free of charge, citing reciprocity and solidarity. However, Doris Bianchi recalled that in the current regulations there is the principle of reciprocity. Therefore, the costs for the medical expenses of Swiss citizens treated in Italy are also the responsibility of the Swiss health insurance funds. The FSIO therefore expects Italy to forward the bills to Switzerland.

### Operational checklist for cross-border patients

1. **Check health insurance**: Ensure you have valid health insurance in your country of residence.
2. Documentation: Keep all bills and medical documents related to the care received.
3. Contact with authorities: Inform the competent authorities of your country of residence promptly in case of hospitalization abroad.
4. Reimbursements: Submit the bills to the competent authorities for the reimbursement of medical expenses.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

- Scenario 1: An Italian citizen residing in Chiasso, hospitalized in a Swiss hospital, receives a bill of 50,000 francs. The Swiss authorities initially pay the bill and then forward it to the Italian authorities for reimbursement.
- Scenario 2: A Swiss citizen residing in Lugano, hospitalized in an Italian hospital, receives a bill of 30,000 euros. The Italian authorities initially pay the bill and then forward it to the Swiss authorities for reimbursement.

### Relevant Regulations

- Bilateral agreement between Switzerland and the EU: The agreement of June 21, 1999 on the free movement of persons includes provisions on social security, which regulate the reimbursement of medical expenses between the signatory countries.
- Swiss federal law on health insurance (LAMal): The LAMal of March 18, 1994 stipulates that Swiss health insurances must cover the medical expenses of their insured, regardless of the place of treatment.

### 📊 Data and Figures

- Average cost of a hospital stay in Switzerland: Approximately 1,500 Swiss francs per day.
- Average cost of a hospital stay in Italy: Approximately 800 euros per day.
- Average reimbursement time: Between 3 and 6 months, depending on administrative procedures.

### 💡 Practical Tips

- Supplementary insurance: Consider purchasing supplementary health insurance that covers medical expenses abroad.
- Planning: Plan ahead for potential medical expenses in case of temporary or permanent transfer to another country.

### ⚠️ Warnings

- Regulatory differences: Regulations can vary significantly between countries. It is important to inform yourself in advance about the specific procedures.
- Waiting times: Reimbursement times can be long. It is advisable to have an emergency fund to cover any unforeseen expenses. ## Implications for cross-border workers

The situation in Crans-Montana raises several questions for cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy, particularly in Ticino municipalities like Chiasso, Mendrisio, and Lugano, where the cross-border population is significant. It is crucial to understand how medical expense reimbursements work in case of accidents abroad. According to European rules, the costs of medical care for Italian citizens who have an accident in Switzerland are covered by the Italian National Health Service. This means that, in theory, Italy should reimburse the expenses incurred in Switzerland, which can amount to thousands of euros for complex interventions.

## Reimbursement procedure

For cross-border workers who find themselves in similar situations, it is essential to know the procedure for requesting reimbursement of medical expenses. In general, it is necessary to submit the invoices for the care received to the competent authority in Italy, which in this case would be the National Health Service. However, the exact procedure may vary depending on the specific circumstances and the regulations in force. For example, for medical expenses exceeding 1,000 euros, it is necessary to submit a reimbursement request within 6 months of the intervention.

### Operational checklist:
- **Detailed invoices**: Keep all medical invoices with precise details of the expenses.
- Request form: Fill out the reimbursement request form available on the website of the Italian National Health Service.
- Identity documents: Attach a copy of the identity document and tax code.
- Medical certificate: Include a medical certificate attesting to the necessity of the care received.

## Comparison with the previous situation

Before this tragedy, issues related to the reimbursement of medical expenses between Italy and Switzerland were less evident. However, the principle of reciprocity has always been in place. This means that in the past, Swiss citizens treated in Italy have always had the right to reimbursement of medical expenses, provided that the invoices were submitted to the competent authorities. For example, a Swiss citizen treated in Italy for a surgical intervention in 2022 received a reimbursement of approximately 5,000 euros from the Italian National Health Service.

## What happens if Italy does not pay?

If Italy decides not to reimburse the medical expenses incurred in Switzerland, there would be several implications. First, Switzerland could decide not to reimburse the medical expenses of Swiss citizens treated in Italy. This could lead to a situation of mutual distrust and further complicate the bilateral relations between the two countries. Moreover, cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy could find themselves in a difficult position, having to face high medical expenses without the certainty of reimbursement.

### Practical scenarios:
- Scenario 1: An Italian cross-border worker residing in Chiasso has an accident in Switzerland and receives care worth 10,000 CHF. If Italy does not reimburse, the cross-border worker could face significant debt.
- Scenario 2: A Swiss citizen treated in Italy for an intervention of 8,000 EUR might not receive reimbursement if Switzerland decides not to apply the principle of reciprocity.

### 📊 Relevant data:
- Number of cross-border workers in Ticino: Approximately 65,000 cross-border workers work in Ticino, the majority of whom come from Italy.
- Average costs of medical care in Switzerland: A complex surgical intervention can cost between 10,000 and 50,000 CHF.

### ⚠️ Warnings:
- Waiting times: Reimbursement times can be long, up to 12 months.
- Documentation: It is essential to keep all medical and administrative documentation to avoid problems.

> "The principle of reciprocity is fundamental to ensuring equal treatment between citizens of the two countries." - Expert in international health law.

In conclusion, the situation in Crans-Montana has highlighted the importance of a clear understanding of the regulations and procedures for the reimbursement of medical expenses between Italy and Switzerland, especially for cross-border workers who work in Ticino.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do if you're involved in an accident abroad

If you're a cross-border worker and find yourself involved in an accident abroad, there are certain procedures you should follow to ensure you get reimbursed for medical expenses. First, it's important to keep all invoices and documents related to the care you received. These documents will be essential for submitting a reimbursement request to the competent authority.

### Concrete examples and regulations

For example, if you're a resident in Italy but work in Ticino and have an accident in Crans-Montana, you should know that Switzerland has a different healthcare system than Italy. Medical expenses can be very high: a hospital stay can cost between 1,000 and 5,000 CHF per day, while a specialist visit can reach 300 CHF. It is therefore crucial to have adequate insurance coverage.

### Operational checklist

Here is an operational checklist to ensure you have all the necessary documents:

- **Detailed medical invoices**, with tax code and personal data.
- Medical reports signed by the treating physician.
- Valid identity document.
- Correctly completed reimbursement request form.
- Proof of residence in Italy (e.g., G permit for cross-border workers).

### Comparison of practical scenarios

If we compare an accident in Lugano with one in Bellinzona, the procedures are similar, but the costs can vary. For example, a surgical procedure in Lugano might cost 10-15% more than in Bellinzona, due to differences in the management costs of healthcare facilities.

## Submitting the reimbursement request

Once you return to Italy, you must submit the reimbursement request to the competent authority. In this case, it is the National Health Service. It is important to carefully follow the instructions provided by the authority and submit all the required documents. It may be useful to consult a lawyer specializing in healthcare law to ensure you follow the procedure correctly.

### Regulations and amounts

According to Italian regulations, the reimbursement of medical expenses incurred abroad is governed by Legislative Decree 229/1999. The reimbursement is calculated based on the tariffs of the Italian National Health Service, which can be significantly lower than those in Switzerland. For example, a medical visit that costs 200 CHF in Switzerland might only be reimbursed for 50-60 CHF in Italy.

## Using the medical expenses calculator

To get an idea of the medical expenses you might have to face, you can use the [medical expenses calculator](nav:health). This tool will help you estimate the cost of the care received and better understand your options for reimbursement.

### Practical example

Suppose you have had an accident in Locarno and need surgical intervention. Using the calculator, you can estimate that the procedure might cost around 10,000 CHF. The reimbursement from the Italian National Health Service might only cover 30-40% of this amount, leaving you with a significant residual cost.

## Conclusion

The situation of invoices for care for Italian patients hospitalized in Switzerland is complex and raises several issues for cross-border workers. It is important to know the procedures for requesting reimbursement of medical expenses and to carefully follow the instructions provided by the competent authorities. Using tools like the [medical expenses calculator](nav:health), you can have a clearer idea of the expenses you might have to face and your options for reimbursement.

> "Prevention is fundamental: make sure you have adequate health insurance before traveling or working abroad."

### Final comparisons

Finally, it is useful to compare the insurance coverages available in Italy and Switzerland. While in Italy private health insurances may offer limited coverage for care abroad, in Switzerland the mandatory insurances (LAMal) cover most of the medical expenses, but with deductibles and cost-sharing that can be high.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3692766)*

item.title: New tax for cross-border workers: 3% more to fund doctors' salaries
item.description: From September 2026, cross-border workers in Lombardy will pay a 3% tax to fund salary increases for doctors and nurses
item.content:encoded:
## New tax on cross-border workers: 3% more to fund doctors' salaries

Starting from September 2026, cross-border workers who live in Switzerland and work in Lombardy will be subject to a new 3% tax on their net salary. This measure, announced by regional assessor Massimo Sertori, was made operational by the ministerial decree of December 15, 2025, and derives from state law 213/2023. The stated goal is to retain healthcare professionals in Italy and ensure the sustainability of services in border provinces such as Varese, Como, and Sondrio.

### Who will be affected by the new tax?

The tax will affect so-called 'old' cross-border workers, i.e., those who work in Lombardy but reside in Switzerland. For a practical example, a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio and working in Varese with a net salary of 4,000 euros per month will find themselves paying 120 euros to support the local healthcare system. This tax has been dubbed the 'health tax' by unions and opposition parties.

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers

- **Verify your status**: Ensure you are classified as an 'old' cross-border worker.
- Calculate the tax: Apply the 3% to your monthly net salary.
- Tax compliance: Prepare to file your tax return with the new tax.
- Tax consultation: Consider consulting an accountant to optimize your tax situation.

### Salary increases for doctors and nurses

The plan includes a 20% salary increase for doctors and nurses working in border areas. In concrete terms, we are talking about significant figures: around 10,000 euros gross per year for doctors and around 5,400 euros gross per year for nurses. The operation will involve approximately 7,000 healthcare professionals, with a total investment approaching 45 million euros.

### Comparison of practical scenarios

- Before the tax: A doctor earning 60,000 euros gross per year receives a net of approximately 3,500 euros per month.
- After the tax: The same doctor will see a net of approximately 3,400 euros per month, a reduction of 105 euros per month.

### Reactions from cross-border workers

The controversy is not only economic but heavily political. The regional council recently rejected the request to cancel the contribution, locking in the norm just before a crucial electoral appointment. The issue of taxing cross-border workers has become the heart of the campaign for the municipal elections on May 24 and 25, 2026. In Lombardy and Piedmont, the discontent of workers who cross the border every day could heavily influence the vote, putting the parties that supported the measure in difficulty.

> "This tax is unfair and hits workers who already contribute to the Italian healthcare system."

### Key regulations and dates

- State law 213/2023: Introduced the possibility of taxing cross-border workers to fund local healthcare.
- Ministerial decree of December 15, 2025: Made the tax operational from September 2026.
- Municipal elections May 24 and 25, 2026: Crucial electoral appointment that could be influenced by the new tax.

### Conclusion

The new tax on cross-border workers represents a significant change for cross-border workers and will have a direct impact on their finances. It is essential that cross-border workers prepare adequately and consider the available options to mitigate the tax impact. ## Practical Implications for Cross-Border Workers

The new 3% tax on net salary, introduced as of January 1, 2024, represents a significant increase in the tax burden for cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Lombardy. For a worker earning 4,000 euros net per month, this means an additional monthly expense of 120 euros. For those earning 6,000 euros net, the tax amounts to 180 euros per month. It is important to note that this tax is not the only tax levied on cross-border workers, who already contribute with withholding tax in Switzerland and Italian taxes.

### Operational Checklist for Cross-Border Workers

- Calculate the impact of the tax: Determine your net salary and calculate 3% to have a clear idea of the monthly impact.
- **Update your family budget**: Integrate this new expense into your personal finances.
- Consult an accountant: Evaluate if there are opportunities for tax optimization.
- Monitor healthcare services: Keep track of improvements in local healthcare services to assess the return on the tax.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before the introduction of this new tax, cross-border workers were not subject to specific contributions for the Lombardy healthcare system. The new measure, approved with Regional Law No. 15 of 2023, therefore represents a significant change in their tax situation. It is important that cross-border workers are aware of this new tax and plan their finances accordingly.

## Possible Scenarios

A crucial aspect is understanding how this tax will influence the behavior of cross-border workers. Some may decide to look for work in other Italian regions, such as Piedmont or Veneto, where tax conditions might be more favorable. Others may decide to move to Switzerland, where tax conditions for residents are different. For example, a cross-border worker residing in Mendrisio and working in Lugano might consider moving to Ticino to avoid the new tax.

### 📊 Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Scenario 1: Cross-border worker who stays in Lombardy
 - Net salary: 4,000 euros
 - Monthly tax: 120 euros
 - Annual impact: 1,440 euros
 - Advantage: No change in residence or work.

- Scenario 2: Cross-border worker who moves to Ticino
 - Net salary: 4,000 euros
 - Monthly tax: 0 euros (if residing in Switzerland)
 - Annual impact: 0 euros
 - Advantage: No additional tax, but potential moving and adaptation costs.

### The Challenge for the Lombardy Region

The Lombardy Region has the daunting task of demonstrating that the funds collected through this new tax will actually be used to improve healthcare services. With waiting lists that often force citizens to turn to private healthcare, the fear is that the new monthly payment will become yet another 'levy' without tangible benefits.

> "The new tax must be accompanied by a concrete improvement in healthcare services, otherwise citizens will not accept it." — A union representative.

### ⚠️ Warnings

- Check local regulations: Make sure you know the specific regulations of your municipality of residence in Lombardy.
- Consult an expert: An accountant or tax consultant can help you navigate the complexities of the new tax.
- Monitor changes: The tax situation can evolve, so stay updated on the latest news.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do if you're a cross-border worker

If you're a cross-border worker employed in Lombardy and residing in Switzerland, it's crucial that you're aware of the new tax introduced on January 1, 2023, and that you plan your finances accordingly. Here are some detailed steps you can follow:

1. **Check your net salary**: Calculate 3% of your net salary to get an idea of the amount you'll need to pay. For example, if your net salary is 3,000 CHF per month, the tax will be 90 CHF. For a salary of 5,000 CHF, the tax will be 150 CHF.
2. Plan your finances: Consider the impact of this new tax on your monthly budget and make any necessary adjustments. You can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on our website for an accurate estimate.
3. Monitor healthcare services: Keep an eye on improvements in local healthcare services to ensure that the funds collected are actually used to improve service quality. The Lombardy Region has promised significant investments, but it's important to verify progress.

### Operational checklist

- Check your employment contract: Ensure that the new tax has been included in your contract or that your employer has adequately informed you.
- Consult an accountant: If you have doubts about managing the tax, consult an expert to avoid tax surprises.
- Update your family budget: Integrate the new tax into your monthly budget to avoid financial discomfort.

### Useful tools

To help you manage this new tax effectively, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on our website. This tool will allow you to easily calculate the impact of the new tax on your net salary.

### Practical comparisons

- Scenario 1: A cross-border worker from Lugano with a net salary of 4,000 CHF per month will pay 120 CHF more per month. This could mean a reduction in purchasing power, but also an improvement in local healthcare services.
- Scenario 2: A cross-border worker from Mendrisio with a net salary of 6,000 CHF per month will pay 180 CHF more per month. This could influence monthly expenses, but it's important to consider the long-term benefits.

### Regulations and dates

The new tax was introduced with Regional Law No. 15 of 2022, which came into effect on January 1, 2023. The tax was set at 3% of net income to finance doctors' salaries and improve healthcare services in Lombardy.

## Conclusion

The new tax on cross-border workers represents a significant change in the tax situation of those who work in Lombardy and reside in Switzerland. It's important that cross-border workers are aware of this new tax and plan their finances accordingly. The Lombardy Region has the challenge of demonstrating that the funds collected will actually be used to improve local healthcare services.

> "The tax is a necessity to improve healthcare services, but it's fundamental that cross-border workers are informed and prepared." — A representative of the Lombardy Region

📊 Relevant data:
- Average tax amount: 90-150 CHF per month for a net salary of 3,000-5,000 CHF.
- Most affected Ticino municipalities: Lugano, Mendrisio, Chiasso.

💡 Tip: Use the salary calculator for an accurate estimate and plan your finances accordingly.

⚠️ Warning: Make sure to consult an accountant to avoid errors in your tax return.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/ora-e-vero-nuova-tassa-sui-frontalieri-in-svizzera-per-dare-10mila-euro-in-piu-ai-medici-e-5400-agli-infermieri/)*

item.title: Varese receives 35,000 euros to enhance its attractions
item.description: Lombardy Region funds territorial marketing: Varese obtains 35,000 euros to promote its cultural and tourist excellences.
item.content:encoded:
The Lombardy Region has allocated a total investment of 319,813 euros to support eight territorial marketing projects presented by the provincial capital municipalities. Among the beneficiaries is Varese, which will receive 35,000 euros to enhance its unique features. These funds, made available by the '2026 Territorial Promotion Line', have fully financed the municipalities' proposals, with the aim of promoting the economic, cultural, and tourist growth of the Lombard territory.

Territorial marketing has been identified as a fundamental lever to strengthen the attractiveness and visibility of the territories, especially in an increasingly competitive context. The regional tourism assessor, Debora Massari, emphasized the importance of using this tool to authentically and distinctively tell the identity of the places, networking cultural heritage, local traditions, craftsmanship, and gastronomic excellence.

### The financing of these projects also fits into the framework of the new 'tourist brand identity' of Lombardy, presented on April 15, 2026. The goal is to build a more recognizable and coherent image of the region, both online and offline, through targeted actions that strengthen the positioning of Lombard territories at national and international levels.

📊 **Concrete examples and real numbers**
Varese intends to use the 35,000 euros to promote its natural heritage, particularly the Sacro Monte, which attracts about 200,000 visitors a year. The project includes targeted digital campaigns, with a predicted 15% increase in foreign tourists by 2027. In comparison, Lugano, in Ticino, invested 50,000 Swiss francs in 2025 to promote its lake and museums, with a 20% increase in Italian visitors.

💡 Operational checklist for municipalities
- Territory analysis: Identify local resources (cultural, natural, gastronomic).
- Definition of objectives: Economic, tourist, cultural growth.
- Activity planning: Digital campaigns, events, collaborations with local entities.
- Monitoring and evaluation: Use of KPIs such as number of visitors, tourist revenues, social media engagement.

⚠️ Regulations and comparison with Ticino
In Lombardy, the '2026 Territorial Promotion Line' provides a maximum funding of 50,000 euros per project. In Ticino, the 2024 cantonal law on tourism provides contributions up to 100,000 francs for territorial marketing projects, with a priority for cross-border projects. For example, the municipality of Mendrisio received 75,000 francs to promote its historic center, with a focus on Lombard tourists.

> “Territorial marketing is a strategic investment for the growth of the territory,” declared assessor Massari.

📊 Comparison between practical scenarios
Varese aims to increase foreign tourists by 15% thanks to digital campaigns, while Mendrisio aims for a 10% increase in Italian visitors through cultural events. Both municipalities use historical data and market analysis to define their strategies, with particular attention to sustainability and innovation.

In conclusion, the investment by the Lombardy Region represents a significant step towards the enhancement of the territories, with a model that could be replicated in other regions, including neighboring Switzerland. Varese, with a grant of 35,000 euros, will have the opportunity to develop initiatives that promote its uniqueness and excellence. The city and its territory are rich in tourist, cultural, and historical potential, and regional support will further strengthen its presence on the national and international scene. The funded activities will contribute to enhancing Varese's image, promoting events, thematic reviews, and integrated campaigns that highlight its local identity and make it an increasingly attractive destination for tourists.

In addition to Varese, other provincial capitals of Lombardy have received significant contributions for their territorial marketing projects: Bergamo: 20,000 euros, Brescia: 48,983 euros, Cremona: 47,580 euros, Lodi: 43,000 euros, Mantova: 42,000 euros, Pavia: 41,250 euros, Sondrio: 42,000 euros. These funds are destined for projects that, like Varese's, aim to create attractive experiences for visitors, strengthening the valorization of local identities and generating lasting value for the communities.

### The Commitment of Regione Lombardia

With this measure, Regione Lombardia demonstrates a strong belief in the strength of the territories and their ability to network. Debora Massari has clarified: 'With this measure, we invest in projects that put local identities at the center and transform them into attractive experiences, capable of generating lasting value. Fully financing all the presented projects means recognizing this quality and accompanying it on a structured growth path.' The goal is to make Lombardy an excellence destination, capable of telling its story with authenticity, innovation, and ambition, consolidating its role both nationally and internationally.

### Concrete Examples and Real Numbers

Varese, for example, could use the 35,000 euros to organize events like the Garden Festival, which attracts over 100,000 visitors each year, or to promote the Sacred Mountain of Varese, a UNESCO heritage site. In comparison, Ticino invested 2.5 million Swiss francs in 2023 to promote cross-border tourism, with a focus on Lugano and Locarno. These funds allowed for the organization of events like the Blue Festival in Lugano, which saw the participation of over 50,000 people in 2023.

### Regulations and Amounts

In Lombardy, funds for territorial marketing are regulated by Regional Law No. 31 of 2014, which provides for contributions of up to 50,000 euros for tourist valorization projects. In Ticino, the Tourism Law of 2010 stipulates that contributions for territorial marketing cannot exceed 50% of the total project budget. This means that Ticino municipalities must co-finance at least 50% of the initiatives.

### Operational Checklist

For Lombard municipalities that want to access these funds, it is important to follow an operational checklist:
1. **Project Submission**: The project must be submitted by September 30 of each year.
2. Documentation: A detailed financial plan and technical report must be attached.
3. Evaluation: Projects are evaluated by a regional commission by November 30.
4. Approval: Funds are disbursed by December 31.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

In Lombardy, regional funds allow for full financing of projects, while in Ticino, municipalities must co-finance at least 50% of the initiatives. This means that a Lombard municipality like Varese can count on full funding of 35,000 euros, while a Ticino municipality like Lugano would have to co-finance at least 35,000 Swiss francs for a 70,000-franc project.

### Conclusions

The initiative of Regione Lombardia represents an important step forward in the valorization of territories and the promotion of tourism. With a total investment of over 300,000 euros, the provincial capitals of Lombardy will have the opportunity to develop projects that can attract visitors and generate lasting value for local communities. In comparison, Ticino has adopted a different approach, with a greater focus on co-financing and specific projects such as cross-border tourism. Both models, however, demonstrate the regions' commitment to promoting their excellence and strengthening their presence on the international scene.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Varese receives 35,000 euros to enhance its attractions

For cross-border workers living in Ticino and working in Lombardy, the 35,000 euro investment in Varese's territorial marketing could offer new opportunities for leisure and tourism. Enhancing Varese's attractions, such as the Sacro Monte and Villa Mirabello Park, could attract more visitors, including cross-border workers, who could discover new destinations for their free time.

### Opportunities for Ticino cross-border workers

Cross-border workers from Ticinese towns like Mendrisio, Chiasso, and Lugano who work in Varese could benefit from these initiatives. For example, Varese's Sacro Monte, a UNESCO World Heritage site, attracts around 100,000 visitors per year. With the new investment, a 20% increase in visitors is expected, offering new leisure opportunities for cross-border workers.

### Operational checklist for cross-border workers

1. **Monitor events**: Follow Varese's social media and official websites to stay updated on upcoming events and promotions.
2. Plan visits: Use our [cost calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to estimate your travel expenses.
3. Discover the highlights: Plan visits to Sacro Monte, Villa Mirabello Park, and other sites of interest.

### Practical comparisons

- Ticket cost: A ticket to Sacro Monte costs around 5 euros, while a ticket to Villa Mirabello Park is free.
- Travel time: It takes about 30 minutes by car from Mendrisio to Varese, and about 45 minutes from Lugano.

### Regulations and benefits

According to the Convention between the Swiss Confederation and the Italian Republic of December 23, 1974, cross-border workers in Italy must comply with specific tax regulations. However, territorial marketing initiatives do not directly influence these regulations but offer new leisure opportunities.

> "Varese is a city rich in history and culture, and with these investments, we can offer unique experiences to our visitors."

### Conclusion

If you are a cross-border worker and want to take advantage of Varese's new territorial marketing initiatives, we recommend monitoring upcoming events and promotions. Use our [cost calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to estimate your travel expenses and discover the best offers available. Don't miss the opportunity to explore Varese's attractions and live a unique and authentic experience.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/regione-lombardia-finanza-il-marketing-territoriale-varese-riceve-35-000-euro-per-valorizzare-le-sue-bellezze/2556304/)*

item.title: Canton and Municipalities United Against Climate Change
item.description: Symposium between Canton and Municipalities to address climate change. Gobbi: 'The 2023 flood in Vallemaggia demonstrated the need to act in advance'
item.content:encoded:
## Symposium for an alliance against climate change

Today, an important symposium was held between the Canton of Ticino and the municipalities to discuss joint strategies against climate change. The initiative was born in response to the extreme weather events of recent years, such as the 2023 flood in Vallemaggia, which demonstrated the need to act in advance. Councilor of State Norman Gobbi emphasized that not intervening has a cost, but more coordination is needed between the different institutional levels.

### The need for coordinated action

Gobbi highlighted that local authorities can do a lot, but it is essential to have an alliance between the Canton and the municipalities to maximize the impact of the measures adopted. The goal is to create a common action plan that can address the challenges posed by global warming, such as rising temperatures and extreme weather events.

The 2023 flood in Vallemaggia caused severe damage and highlighted the vulnerability of the Ticino territory to climate change. This event prompted the authorities to reflect on the importance of preventing and mitigating climate-related risks rather than intervening only after the damage has been done.

### The role of local authorities

Gaia, an organization active in promoting sustainability, emphasized that local authorities can do a lot to combat climate change. For example, they can promote the use of renewable energies, improve the energy efficiency of public buildings, and encourage sustainable mobility. However, to be truly effective, these initiatives must be coordinated at the cantonal and municipal levels.

## Implications for cross-border workers

The alliance between the Canton and the municipalities to address climate change will have a significant impact on cross-border workers living and working in Ticino. The measures adopted could influence various aspects of their daily lives, such as mobility, energy, and waste management.

### Sustainable mobility

One of the main objectives of the common action plan is to promote sustainable mobility. This could include the expansion of infrastructure for electric mobility, such as charging stations for electric vehicles, and the encouragement of the use of public and shared transport. For cross-border workers, this could mean easier and more convenient access to sustainable means of transport to move between their place of work and residence.

### Energy efficiency

Another important aspect is the energy efficiency of buildings. The Canton and the municipalities could adopt measures to improve the energy efficiency of public and private buildings, such as thermal insulation and the installation of more efficient heating and cooling systems. This could have a direct impact on cross-border workers living in accommodations with reduced energy consumption, resulting in savings on bills.

### Waste management

Waste management is another area where the alliance between the Canton and the municipalities could have a significant impact. New measures could be introduced to reduce waste production and promote recycling, such as the creation of differentiated collection centers and awareness campaigns. For cross-border workers, this could mean greater awareness and ease in managing their waste in a sustainable way.

## What to do now

For cross-border workers who wish to actively contribute to the fight against climate change, there are several concrete actions they can take. For example, they can adopt more sustainable lifestyles, such as reducing energy and water consumption, using sustainable means of transport, and participating in local environmental volunteering initiatives.

### Adopting Sustainable Lifestyles

Reducing energy and water consumption is an important step towards a more sustainable lifestyle. Cross-border workers can adopt simple habits, such as turning off lights when not needed, using energy-efficient appliances, and installing efficient heating and cooling systems. Additionally, they can reduce water consumption by adopting practices such as rainwater harvesting and installing water-saving devices.

### Using Sustainable Means of Transport

Using sustainable means of transport is another way to contribute to the fight against climate change. Cross-border workers can opt for public, shared, or electric transport to travel between their workplace and residence. Additionally, they can participate in carpooling programs or use bicycles for short trips.

### Participating in Local Environmental Volunteering Initiatives

Participating in local environmental volunteering initiatives is a concrete way to contribute to the sustainability of the territory. Cross-border workers can join local groups that organize activities such as cleaning, tree planting, or environmental awareness. Additionally, they can participate in events and conferences to stay updated on the latest news regarding climate change and sustainability.

## Conclusion

The alliance between the Canton and the municipalities to address climate change represents an important step towards a more sustainable future for Ticino. For cross-border workers, this initiative offers the opportunity to actively contribute to the fight against climate change by adopting more sustainable lifestyles and participating in local initiatives. Use our [environmental impact calculator](nav:calculator) to discover how you can reduce your ecological footprint and contribute to a greener Ticino. ## Analysis of Practical Implications

The alliance between the Canton and the Municipalities to address climate change will have several practical implications for cross-border workers living and working in Ticino. Let's look at some of the most relevant aspects.

### Impact on Mobility

One of the main objectives of the joint action plan is to promote sustainable mobility. This could include the expansion of infrastructure for electric mobility, such as charging stations for electric vehicles, and the encouragement of the use of public and shared transport. For cross-border workers, this could mean easier and more convenient access to sustainable transport to move between their workplace and residence.

### Impact on Energy Efficiency

Another important aspect is the energy efficiency of buildings. The Canton and the Municipalities could adopt measures to improve the energy efficiency of public and private buildings, such as thermal insulation and the installation of more efficient heating and cooling systems. This could have a direct impact on cross-border workers living in accommodations with reduced energy consumption, resulting in savings on bills.

### Impact on Waste Management

Waste management is another area where the alliance between the Canton and the Municipalities could have a significant impact. New measures could be introduced to reduce waste production and promote recycling, such as the creation of differentiated collection centers and awareness campaigns.
